- These are not perfect apples-to-apples comparisons (different years, samples, definitions).
- But they consistently suggest a harsh baseline:
  - Roughly **only ~20%–30%** of active day traders are profitable
  - Roughly **~70%–80%** lose money

Practical implication (the speaker’s framing):
- Ask yourself honestly: *If we take 100 traders, am I likely to be in the top 22–27?*
- Many people assume they will be the exception—statistics say most won’t.

---

concepts.title: 3) Incentives: Why Platforms and “Easy Money” Marketing Can Mislead
concepts.body:
The speaker highlights a structural issue:

- Brokerages/platforms earn from **activity**:
  - More trades → more **commissions/spreads/fees** (depending on the platform model)
- This doesn’t mean platforms “want you to lose,” but they have **no incentive to discourage frequent trading**.

Red flag marketing claims:
- “Transfer your money to us—our **algo always works**.”
- “Secret formula—can’t share.”
- The speaker argues:
  - If an algorithm were consistently extraordinary, large institutions (e.g., major banks/investment firms) would likely **buy it** or replicate it.
  - “Always works” is usually unrealistic because markets change and edges decay.

Actionable lesson:
- Treat “guaranteed,” “always,” and “secret system” claims as **high-risk signals**.

---

concepts.title: 4) Why Day Trading Is Hard: The Core Failure Modes
concepts.body:
#### A) **Volatility** (the same thing that creates opportunity creates danger)
- **Volatility** = how much price moves up/down.
- Example given: a stock can open **+5%** and finish **–5%** in the same day.
- For day traders:
  - Volatility is the *source of profit potential*
  - But also the *source of rapid losses*

#### B) **Emotions** (humans aren’t computers)
- The speaker emphasizes that traders are not “cold” like a machine.
- Emotional traps:
  - Panic selling
  - FOMO buying
  - “Revenge trading” after losses (trying to “get it back” quickly)
- A key point: repeated losses can become psychologically paralyzing, leading to worse decisions.

#### C) Lack of “verification” / limited margin for error
- Intraday setups are often a small number of “clear” trades.
- If the trade goes against you, there may be no portfolio diversification or long time horizon to “balance it out.”
- This increases reliance on:
  - precise execution
  - strict risk control
  - consistent edge

#### D) **Lack of knowledge** and speed of decision-making
- Day trading is compared to **driving Formula 1**:
  - You don’t learn to drive for the first time in an F1 car.
- In day trading, everything happens faster:
  - reading charts
  - interpreting candles
  - executing entries/exits
  - managing risk in real time

---

concepts.title: 5) Traits and Skills Required (Self-Assessment Framework)
concepts.body:
The speaker lists key traits and turns them into a personal “test.”

#### Core traits
- **Patience**: Some days you do nothing because there is no good setup.
  - Professional traders may “sit on their hands.”
- **Discipline**: Following rules even when emotions push you to break them.
- **Risk management**: Especially the ability to keep a **stop-loss** and not move it “just a little.”
- **Adaptability**: Markets change; being rigid (“today the market will crash”) can be costly.
- **Technical analysis skill**: Reading charts quickly and accurately.
- **Emotional control**: Handling losses without spiraling into revenge trades.

---

concepts.title: 6) The 10-Question Readiness Test (What to Ask Yourself)
concepts.body:
The speaker suggests taking a screenshot and answering privately and honestly. Key questions include:

1) Do I have a solid understanding of **financial markets** and how they work?
   - Do I know what reports/events come out and when?
2) Do I have strong experience in **technical analysis** and chart reading?
   - He suggests a benchmark: if your “correct reads” are under **~70%**, keep practicing before using real money.
3) Am I comfortable taking **large risk** to earn?
   - Example framing: risking **thousands of dollars** on a trade (depending on strategy).
4) Do I have a **trading plan**, and do I follow it?
5) Can I build a plan in a practical way (notes, Excel, etc.)?
6) Do I have the **discipline** to stick to the plan?
   - How often have you said “I’ll sell” and didn’t?
   - How often did you skip a stop-loss?
7) Can I adapt to **changing market conditions**?
8) Do I have enough **capital** to support this activity?
   - Warning: if your plan is “I’ll quit work and day trade,” be careful—this is a major risk signal.
9) Do I understand **risk management** well?
10) Am I willing to invest the **time and effort** required?
   - Practical reality (for Israeli viewers trading US markets): the day can run roughly **15:00–23:00** local time, consuming afternoons/evenings.

Important caveat:
- There is **no guaranteed score** that makes you a successful trader.
- Even “yes” to everything doesn’t remove risk.

---

concepts.title: 7) What to Do If You Still Want to Day Trade (Safer Preparation Steps)
concepts.body:
#### Step 1: **Educate yourself continuously**
- Learn market mechanics, strategies, and how/why setups work.
- Prefer serious material over “storytelling” or hype.

#### Step 2: Build a **Trading Plan**
A plan should define:
- What you trade (stocks? which types?)
- When you trade (hours, days)
- Your setup rules (entry triggers)
- Your exit rules (profit target, stop-loss)
- Position sizing rules (how much you risk per trade)

Concrete example from the transcript:
- A professional day trader writes each morning (even on a simple note) **3–4 tickers** to focus on.
- Purpose: reduce randomness and force discipline.

#### Step 3: Practice without money using charts (even without a broker demo)
The speaker suggests a practical method:
- Use **TradingView** (even free) and practice on lower timeframes:
  - **1 minute, 5 minutes, 15 minutes** (depending on your style)
- “Draw the line” (mark levels), then write a mock trade:
  - Entry: e.g., “I would enter at 1.30”
  - **Stop-loss**: e.g., “1.00”
  - **Take-profit**: e.g., “1.01” (example numbers were illustrative)
- Track outcomes in a notebook/Excel:
  - How often did the setup work?
  - Are you improving your hit rate and execution?

Key idea:
- Before practicing order execution in a broker **demo account**, first prove you can identify setups consistently.

#### Step 4: Start small and avoid greed
- Don’t begin with large size.
- The goal early is process consistency, not income replacement.

#### Step 5: Risk management is non-negotiable
- The speaker repeatedly highlights the common failure:
  - moving the stop-loss
  - refusing to take a loss
  - flipping bias too quickly without rules (“now I’ll short!”)

---

concepts.title: 8) Lifestyle and Time Commitment (Often Ignored)
concepts.body:
Day trading isn’t just “a strategy,” it’s a schedule:
- If you trade US markets from Israel, it can dominate:
  - afternoons/evenings
  - potentially Fridays into the weekend (depending on season/market hours)
- This matters for:
  - family time
  - religious observance
  - work compatibility
  - mental fatigue

---

takeaways.text: Use base rates: assume **~70%–80%** of active day traders lose money unless you can prove you’re different with data.
Avoid “algo always works” and “secret formula” marketing—treat as a red flag.
Day trading requires:
**Patience** (no trade is a valid decision)
**Discipline** (follow rules fast)
**Risk management** (stop-loss discipline)
**Technical analysis skill**
**Emotional control**
Don’t fund day trading until you can:
write a clear **trading plan**
demonstrate consistent results in **paper practice** (tracked, not “in your head”)
keep losses small and controlled
Reality-check capital and lifestyle:
Do you have enough **capital**?
Can you commit the **time window** consistently?

glossary.definition: Buying and selling within the same trading day.
The magnitude of price movement up and down.
Written rules for entries, exits, risk, and what/when to trade.
A predefined exit level to cap losses if price moves against you.
Position sizing and exit discipline to prevent a few losses from wiping out the account.
Analyzing price charts (candles, trends, levels) to make trading decisions.
Simulated trading to test a strategy without risking real money.

title: Understanding the 11 S&P 500 Sectors (and How to Invest in Them with ETFs)
learn_items: How the **S&P 500** is divided into **11 sectors**, and why that classification exists.
How to gain targeted exposure to a sector using **sector ETFs** (instead of picking single stocks).
How to evaluate a sector ETF using practical metrics: **AUM**, **dividend yield**, and **top holdings concentration**.
Why sector performance differs dramatically over time (with real examples like **XLK vs. XLC**).

concepts.title: ### 1) Why “Sectors” Exist (and why investors use them)
concepts.body:
- The **S&P 500** contains ~500 large US companies, but they don’t all behave the same.
- To make analysis and portfolio construction easier, companies are grouped into **sectors** (business categories).
- Sectors help you:
  - Understand what’s driving index performance (e.g., tech vs. energy).
  - Adjust portfolio exposure without stock-picking (e.g., “I want more energy”).
  - Compare performance across parts of the economy.

**Heatmap idea (conceptually):**
- A common way to visualize the S&P 500 is a “market heatmap,” where:
  - Each company’s box size reflects its **market cap** (bigger companies = bigger influence).
  - This reinforces that the index is **market-cap weighted** (not equal-weighted).

---

concepts.title: ### 2) The “Exposure Pyramid” (broad index → narrower bets)
concepts.body:
The transcript describes a practical way to think about risk/exposure:
- Broad exposure: **S&P 500** (diversified baseline)
- Then possibly other broad indices (e.g., Nasdaq, Russell)
- Narrower exposure: **Sector ETFs**
- Even narrower: individual stocks (highest specificity / higher single-name risk)

**Key lesson:** Sector ETFs are a middle ground—more targeted than the S&P 500, but less risky than betting on one company.

---

concepts.title: ### 3) The 11 S&P 500 Sectors and Their ETF Tickers
concepts.body:
The video focuses on the common US sector ETF set (often called “Select Sector SPDRs”):

- **XLB** — Materials  
- **XLC** — Communication Services  
- **XLE** — Energy  
- **XLF** — Financials  
- **XLI** — Industrials  
- **XLK** — Technology  
- **XLP** — Consumer Staples  
- **XLRE** — Real Estate  
- **XLU** — Utilities  
- **XLV** — Health Care  
- **XLY** — Consumer Discretionary  

**Important:** There are also “sub-sector” ETFs (e.g., cybersecurity, clean energy), but this lesson is about the S&P’s main 11-sector structure.

---

concepts.title: ### 4) Sector performance is NOT the same (and changes over time)
concepts.body:
The instructor compares sector returns over different time windows to show that:
- Different sectors can have wildly different returns over the same period.
- **Past performance does not guarantee future performance**, but it helps you understand behavior and risk.

**Example from the transcript (5-year performance):**
- **XLC (Communication Services)**: about **+2.3%** over 5 years (very weak)
- **XLK (Technology)**: about **+119%** over 5 years (very strong)

**Example from the transcript (Year-to-date):**
- Many sectors were negative YTD, while **XLE (Energy)** was positive/strong relative to others.

**Practical takeaway:** Sector rotation happens. What leads in one period can lag in another.

---

concepts.title: ### 5) How to analyze a Sector ETF (a repeatable framework)
concepts.body:
The instructor emphasizes starting with the ETF’s “facts,” not the chart.

Use this checklist-style approach:

#### A) **AUM / Total Assets**
- **AUM (Assets Under Management)** shows the size of the fund.
- Larger AUM often implies:
  - Better liquidity (easier trading)
  - More stability of the product
  - Tighter spreads (often, not always)

**Examples:**
- **XLE**: about **$34B** AUM (very large)
- **XLF**: about **$28B** AUM
- **XLB**: about **$4.8B** AUM (smaller)

#### B) **Dividend Yield**
- **Dividend yield** shown is typically **annualized** (yearly), even though distributions are often paid quarterly.
- Sector ETFs distribute dividends they receive from their holdings:
  - The ETF collects dividends from the underlying companies
  - Then pays them out to ETF shareholders

**Examples:**
- **XLE** dividend yield: about **4.2%**
- **XLF** dividend yield: about **2.7%**
- **XLC** dividend yield: about **0.16%** (very low—many holdings don’t pay meaningful dividends)

**Key insight:** If the underlying companies don’t pay dividends, the ETF can’t “create” dividends.

#### C) **Holdings + Concentration Risk**
- Look at:
  - Number of holdings
  - Weight of the top 1–2 companies
- A sector ETF can be “diversified” in number of names but still **concentrated** if the top holdings dominate performance.

**Example: XLE concentration**
- **Exxon Mobil (XOM)** ~**23%**
- **Chevron (CVX)** ~**19%**
- Together ~**42%** of the ETF → very top-heavy

**Meaning:** If Exxon/Chevron fall, XLE likely falls heavily even if smaller holdings do fine.

#### D) Where to find holdings
- You can:
  - Use an ETF data site (the instructor uses one for convenience)
  - Or simply Google: **“XLE holdings”**, **“XLF holdings”**, etc. (often leads to MarketWatch or issuer pages)

---

concepts.title: ### 6) Sector walk-throughs (what’s inside + what it implies)
**XLE — Energy**
concepts.body:
What it’s for:
- Broad exposure to energy companies without choosing a single oil/gas stock.

What the instructor highlights:
- Large AUM (~$34B)
- High dividend yield (~4.2%)
- Very concentrated in top names:
  - Exxon, Chevron, then others like ConocoPhillips, EOG, Pioneer, Schlumberger, Marathon, etc.

**Investor lesson:** XLE is a sector bet, but also heavily a bet on its top two giants.

---

concepts.title: **XLF — Financials**
concepts.body:
What it’s for:
- Exposure to banks and financial institutions without picking one bank.

Key stats mentioned:
- AUM ~ $28B
- Dividend yield ~ 2.7% (annualized)

**Holdings insight (important “trick”):**
- The largest holding is **Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B)** (~14%).
- Berkshire’s largest holding is **Apple**—so indirectly, Apple influences XLF through Berkshire.
  - This is a real-world example of “look-through exposure”: sector ETFs can contain conglomerates that bring in exposure you might not expect.

Other major holdings mentioned:
- JPMorgan, Bank of America, Wells Fargo
- Also: Charles Schwab, Morgan Stanley, S&P Global, Goldman Sachs, BlackRock, American Express, Citigroup, etc.

**Sub-sector idea inside Financials:**
- Banks vs. capital markets vs. insurance vs. financial services can behave differently.
- You can examine breakdowns if you want more precision.

**Dividend history note:**
- Over long periods, XLF’s dividend yield has been around ~2% on average (varies with price and payouts).

---

concepts.title: **XLB — Materials**
concepts.body:
What it’s for:
- Exposure to “inputs” of the economy: chemicals, metals, mining, packaging, construction materials.

Key stats mentioned:
- AUM ~ $4.8B
- Dividend yield ~ 1.38%

Top holdings examples:
- Linde (LIN)
- Air Products & Chemicals
- Sherwin-Williams (paint/coatings)
- Freeport-McMoRan (copper)
- Newmont (gold)
- Albemarle (lithium / battery materials)

**Sub-industries inside Materials (examples mentioned):**
- Chemicals
- Metals & mining
- Containers & packaging
- Construction materials

---

concepts.title: **XLC — Communication Services**
concepts.body:
What it’s for:
- Exposure to major communication/media/internet platforms.

Key stats mentioned:
- AUM around ~$7.8B (as stated)
- Dividend yield ~ 0.16% (very low)

Why performance was weak (example given):
- The ETF is dominated by large names that fell sharply:
  - **Meta** (very large weight; down heavily YTD in the example)
  - **Alphabet (Google)** appears as two share classes; combined weight is very large
  - Netflix, Comcast, Verizon, Disney, etc.

**Key lesson:** Sector ETFs can be dominated by a few mega-caps; if those drop, the sector ETF drops.

**Additional note:**
- XLC is relatively newer as a sector classification (communication services was reorganized vs older “telecom” style groupings), so its long dividend history may be shorter.

---

concepts.title: **XLI — Industrials**
concepts.body:
What it’s for:
- Exposure to “real economy” operators: transportation, machinery, aerospace/defense, logistics, etc.

Key stats mentioned:
- AUM ~ $11.5B
- Dividend yield ~ 1.8%
- Down ~20% YTD in the example period

Holdings examples:
- Union Pacific (railroads)
- Raytheon Technologies (defense)
- UPS (logistics)
- Honeywell
- Deere (machinery/tractors)
- Lockheed Martin (defense)
- Caterpillar, Boeing, General Electric, Northrop Grumman, Waste Management, FedEx, etc.

Sub-sector breakdown example:
- Aerospace & defense (largest weight mentioned)
- Machinery
- Road & rail
- Air freight & logistics
- Electrical equipment
- Commercial services

**Investor lesson:** Industrials can be labor-intensive and cyclical; performance may depend on economic growth, government spending (defense), and global trade/logistics.

---

takeaways.text: When you want to “increase exposure” to a theme (energy, banks, etc.), consider a **sector ETF** instead of gambling on one stock.
Before buying a sector ETF, check:
**AUM** (size/liquidity)
**Dividend yield** (annualized; depends on holdings)
**Top holdings weights** (concentration risk—top 2 names can dominate)
**Holdings list** (make sure you actually want exposure to those companies)
Don’t assume all sectors perform similarly:
Example: **XLK** massively outperformed **XLC** over 5 years in the video’s data.
Remember “look-through exposure”:
Example: **XLF** holds **Berkshire**, which holds **Apple**—so you may get indirect exposure you didn’t expect.
Use simple research habits:
Google “**[ticker] holdings**” to quickly see what’s inside.

glossary.definition: A category grouping companies with similar business activities (e.g., Energy, Financials).
An exchange-traded fund designed to track one sector (e.g., XLE tracks Energy).
A market-cap-weighted index of ~500 large US companies.
Company value = share price × shares outstanding; drives weight in market-cap-weighted indices.
Total money invested in a fund; often used as a proxy for size/liquidity.
Annual dividends divided by current price (for ETFs: based on distributions from holdings).
The underlying stocks owned by an ETF.
When a small number of holdings make up a large % of the ETF, heavily influencing returns.
Performance from the start of the current year to today.

title: SPX vs. SPY vs. VOO (and “World Index” ACWI): What You’re Actually Buying
learn_items: The practical difference between **SPX** (the index) and **SPY/VOO** (ETFs that track it).
Why a “global” ETF like **ACWI** may not diversify you as much as you think (because it’s still heavily U.S.-weighted).
How to compare S&P 500 ETFs using **expense ratio**, **trading volume**, and **bid–ask spread**.
When “global diversification” might make sense (and when it’s mostly cosmetic).

concepts.title: 1) “Global diversification” vs. the reality of the S&P 500
concepts.body:
Many investors say: “I don’t want only U.S. stocks—I want global exposure.” The video challenges this idea with a practical point:

- The **S&P 500** contains large U.S.-listed companies, but many are **global businesses**:
  - **Apple** sells worldwide; manufacturing is global (e.g., China/India mentioned).
  - **Microsoft** sells software/cloud globally.
  - **Amazon** operates internationally (shipping/services).
  - **NVIDIA** sells globally (example: discussion about restrictions on sales to China implies global demand).
  - **Google/Meta** are used worldwide.
  - **Tesla** has factories and sales globally (Shanghai, Berlin, U.S. mentioned).
  - Payments networks like **Visa/Mastercard** are global.

**Core lesson:**  
Even if companies are *legally U.S.-listed*, their **revenues and operations are global**, so the S&P 500 is not “U.S.-only” in an economic sense.

#### Concentration inside the S&P 500
- The video notes the **top 10 holdings** are about **~32%** of the S&P 500.
- This matters because:
  - Your performance is heavily influenced by a small group of mega-cap companies.
  - “Diversification” within the index is not equal-weight; it’s **market-cap weighted**.

---

concepts.title: 2) What ACWI is—and why it may not diversify much
concepts.body:
**ACWI** (iShares MSCI ACWI ETF) is presented as a popular “world index” ETF.

#### What’s inside ACWI?
When the presenter checks the largest holdings of **ACWI**, the top positions look very similar to the S&P 500:
- Apple, Microsoft, Amazon, NVIDIA, Google, Meta, etc.

He then scrolls until he finds major non-U.S. names (examples mentioned):
- **Novo Nordisk**
- **Nestlé**
- **Samsung**
- **LVMH** (noted with a small weight around **0.33%**)

**Core lesson:**  
A “global” index can still be dominated by U.S. mega-caps, so the *incremental diversification* you get may be smaller than expected.

---

concepts.title: 3) Performance comparison used in the video (SPY vs. ACWI)
concepts.body:
The video compares returns (with dividends included, per the speaker) and shows ACWI lagging SPY over multiple periods:

- **1 year:** SPY ~**17.9%** vs. ACWI ~**15.1%**
- **Year-to-date:** SPY ~**22%** vs. ACWI ~**17%**
- **5 years:** SPY ~**92%** vs. ACWI ~**63%**

**Interpretation (as taught in the video):**
- If ACWI holds many of the same U.S. leaders but adds more exposure to slower regions, you may end up with **lower returns** without meaningful diversification benefits.
- The presenter argues that adding ACWI can be like “adding an average striker to a championship team”—you dilute performance.

> Important nuance for learners: past performance doesn’t guarantee future results, but the video’s lesson is about understanding what you’re paying for (true diversification vs. “label diversification”).

---

concepts.title: 4) When country-specific investing might matter (but requires expertise)
concepts.body:
The presenter notes that **some countries can outperform strongly** in certain periods, but:
- You need **local economic understanding** (or a clear strategy).
- In a broad global ETF, those countries often have **small weights**, so even big country gains may barely move the total fund.

Examples of strong country ETF moves mentioned (YTD-style examples):
- Poland ETF up ~**45%**
- Greece ~**37%**
- Spain ~**29%**
- Also mentions Ireland/Italy/Germany/France as examples of country exposures

**Core lesson:**  
If you want to benefit from a specific country’s surge, you usually need **targeted country exposure**, not a broad world ETF where that country is a tiny slice.

---

concepts.title: 5) SPX vs. SPY vs. VOO: Index vs. ETFs (the “wrapper” idea)
concepts.body:
#### **SPX** = the index itself (a “table” you can’t buy)
- **SPX** is described as the underlying **S&P 500 index**—a calculated benchmark.
- You **cannot buy SPX directly** like a stock.
- It’s essentially the “scoreboard” / “table” ranking the 500 companies by **market cap weighting**.

#### ETFs = investable “wrappers” that track SPX
Financial firms create **ETFs** that replicate the index:
- They “wrap” the index into a tradable product you can buy/sell.

Two major S&P 500 ETFs discussed:
- **SPY** (SPDR)
- **VOO** (Vanguard)

**Key point:** Their holdings and weights are designed to track the same index, so the main differences are *not* the companies inside, but the trading/fee mechanics.

---

concepts.title: 6) How to choose between SPY and VOO (practical ETF comparison)
concepts.body:
The video highlights three practical comparison factors:

#### A) **Trading volume** (liquidity)
- **SPY** daily volume mentioned: ~**75.8 million shares**
- High volume generally means:
  - Easier to enter/exit positions
  - Better execution for large orders
  - More stable pricing during trading hours

#### B) **Bid–ask spread**
**Bid–ask spread** = the gap between the price you can buy at (ask) and sell at (bid).

- With very liquid ETFs like SPY (and typically VOO), spreads are usually very tight.
- The presenter gives an example:
  - If SPY closes around **460.2**, you can often buy/sell very close to that price intraday.
- In smaller/local ETFs with low volume:
  - The spread can widen, and you might get filled at worse prices (slippage).

**Actionable lesson:**  
For most investors, tight spreads and high liquidity reduce hidden trading costs.

#### C) **Expense ratio** (management fee)
- **SPY expense ratio:** ~**0.09%**
- **VOO expense ratio:** ~**0.02%**

**Interpretation:**
- VOO is cheaper annually.
- But SPY may have superior liquidity (depending on your broker/market hours), which can matter for frequent trading or very large orders.

The presenter’s framing:
- For extremely large portfolios (tens/hundreds of millions), fee differences can become meaningful.
- For typical retail investors, the fee difference may be small in dollar terms, and liquidity/spread may matter more (especially if trading often).

---

concepts.title: 7) Local (Israeli) and Irish-domiciled alternatives: taxes and complexity (high-level)
concepts.body:
The video briefly mentions that beyond U.S.-listed ETFs (like SPY/VOO), investors may consider:
- **Israeli local products** (mutual funds / local index products)
- **Irish-domiciled ETFs** (common among non-U.S. investors)

Topics raised (without deep detail in the transcript):
- **Currency conversion** (USD/ILS handling) may be done inside local products, sometimes with higher fees.
- **U.S. estate tax** risk is mentioned as a concern for non-U.S. holders of U.S.-domiciled assets (the presenter notes he hasn’t personally seen someone pay it, but it exists as a rule).
- Irish ETFs may **reinvest dividends** and can have different tax treatment, but the presenter emphasizes:
  - It’s often overcomplicated
  - The long-term benefit may take many years to become meaningful

**Practical lesson from the tone of the video:**  
Don’t let small structural/tax optimizations distract you from the main drivers: asset allocation, staying invested, costs, and simplicity—unless your situation truly requires it.

---

concepts.title: 8) Why many charts/quotes use SPY (a practical “tracking” tip)
concepts.body:
The presenter explains why he often references **SPY** when discussing the S&P 500:
- SPY price is roughly **~1/10 of SPX** (rule of thumb used in the video).
  - Example: if SPX is ~4604, SPY is ~460.
- This makes it easier to talk about levels and compare moves.

---

takeaways.text: **SPX is the index** (benchmark). You generally **can’t buy it directly** like a stock.
To invest in the S&P 500, use an ETF like **SPY** or **VOO** (they are “wrappers” tracking SPX).
When comparing S&P 500 ETFs, focus on:
**Expense ratio** (VOO ~0.02% vs SPY ~0.09% in the video)
**Liquidity/volume** (SPY extremely high volume ~75.8M shares/day mentioned)
**Bid–ask spread** (tighter is better; reduces hidden costs)
Don’t assume a “world ETF” like **ACWI** gives strong diversification:
Top holdings can still be dominated by U.S. mega-caps
Non-U.S. giants may have **small weights** (example: LVMH ~0.33% mentioned)
If you want to benefit from a specific country’s boom, a broad world ETF may dilute that impact; you may need **country-specific ETFs**—but only if you understand the risks and drivers.
Avoid unnecessary complexity (local vs Irish vs U.S. products) unless taxes/currency/estate issues are truly relevant to your personal situation.

glossary.definition: A calculated benchmark tracking a basket of securities; not necessarily directly investable.
A tradable fund that can track an index (e.g., SPY, VOO).
Companies have weights based on their market value; larger companies influence returns more.
The stocks inside a fund and their percentage allocations.
How much is traded; higher volume usually means easier trading and better pricing.
The difference between buy and sell prices; a hidden cost that matters more in low-liquidity products.
Annual percentage fee charged by the fund, typically embedded in performance.
A potential tax issue for non-U.S. investors holding U.S.-domiciled assets (mentioned as a consideration).

title: Fundamentals (Part 1): How to Learn a Company Before You Touch the Financial Statements
learn_items: How to treat a stock investment like buying a “business asset” (similar to evaluating a rental property).
Why understanding the **business model** comes *before* analyzing cash flow, earnings, and ratios.
A practical method to research any company using its **Investor Relations (IR)** website and **investor presentations**.
Early “go / no-go” filters: when to stop researching because the business doesn’t make sense or doesn’t show growth.

concepts.title: 1) What “Fundamentals” Really Means (Expanded Definition)
concepts.body:
The video reframes **fundamentals** as more than just reading financial statements.

- Traditional view: fundamentals = analyzing **financial data** (cash flow, revenues, etc.).
- Expanded view (in this lesson):
  - Fundamentals also include understanding:
    - What the company *actually does*
    - How it makes money (revenue streams)
    - What drives growth
    - Competitive position (who’s #1 / #2)
    - Whether the story matches reality (avoid being “fooled by numbers”)

Core idea:  
Before you analyze the spreadsheet, you must understand the business well enough to judge whether the numbers are believable and sustainable.

---

concepts.title: 2) Investing Horizon: “Investment” vs “Trade”
concepts.body:
A key rule in the lesson is that your research depth depends on your time horizon.

- **Long-term investment** (rule of thumb in the video): **3–5 years**
  - Requires business understanding + fundamentals.
- **Short-term trade**
  - Different discussion (more about entry/exit, price action, etc.).
  - The speaker implies that if you’re not investing long-term, the “fundamentals deep dive” may not be the main tool.

Practical implication:
- Decide first: *Am I investing for years or trading for weeks/days?*  
  Your entire approach changes.

---

concepts.title: 3) Owning a Stock = Owning a Slice of a Business
concepts.body:
The speaker emphasizes a mindset shift:

- When you buy a **share**, you become a partial owner of the company.
- Management (CEO, board, employees) is incentivized to increase shareholder value because:
  - Public markets provide capital, and
  - There’s an implicit “deal”: investors provide money, the company aims to return more value over time (often via stock price appreciation).

#### Shares Outstanding (Pizza Analogy)
- **Shares outstanding** = total number of shares the company has issued.
- Ownership is like a pizza cut into many slices:
  - If there are many slices, your slice may be small, but it is still ownership.
  - Even **1 / 1,000,000** is still a real fraction of the business.

Why it matters:
- It grounds you in “business owner thinking,” not just “ticker symbol thinking.”

---

concepts.title: 4) The Real Estate Analogy: How to Think Like an Investor
concepts.body:
The lesson compares stock investing to buying an investment property:

When buying a rental property, you check:
- Neighborhood quality
- Building condition
- Apartment details (rooms, rentability)
- Rental strategy (long-term rent vs short-term like Airbnb)
- Area price trends (rising or falling)

Equivalent for stocks:
- Industry and market size (“neighborhood”)
- Business model (“the building”)
- Revenue streams and customer types (“the apartment layout”)
- Growth drivers (“rent demand”)
- Competitive position (“area trends and desirability”)

Key takeaway:  
It’s normal to do deep due diligence on real estate—so it should feel normal to do similar diligence on companies.

---

concepts.title: 5) Step-by-Step: How to Research a Company (Before Financial Statements)
concepts.body:
#### Step 1: Use Google + “IR”
Practical method shown:

- Search: **[Company Name] + IR**
  - Example used: “Shopify IR”
- **IR** = **Investor Relations**
  - The company’s official portal for investors.

What you’re looking for:
- **Investor Presentation** (often a slide deck)
- **Earnings Release** PDFs
- **Earnings Presentation** slides
- Quarterly/annual materials

The speaker demonstrates this works for many companies, not just Shopify:
- Shopify (e-commerce infrastructure)
- Chegg
- Intel
- ZIM (shipping)

#### Step 2: Download and Read the Investor Presentation
Why start here (instead of jumping into income statement/cash flow)?
- Presentations often explain:
  - The business model in plain language
  - Customer segments
  - Product ecosystem
  - Growth strategy
  - Market positioning
  - Key operating metrics and growth charts

Important caution:
- A beautiful presentation is not enough (“nice fonts are nice, but show me the money”).
- Still, it’s the best *first* step to understand what you’re buying.

---

concepts.title: 6) What to Look For Inside an Investor Presentation (Shopify Example)
concepts.body:
Using Shopify’s presentation, the speaker highlights what a beginner should extract:

#### A) Business Model: What the company provides
- Shopify: builds **e-commerce infrastructure** for businesses.
- Not just “they make websites”—you want to understand:
  - How money is earned
  - What products/services drive growth
  - What influences adoption and retention

#### B) Customer Segments
Shopify serves multiple customer types:
- Entrepreneurs / creators
- **SMBs** (small and medium businesses)
- Large brands

Why this matters:
- Different segments have different pricing, churn risk, and growth potential.

#### C) Ecosystem / Moat Signals
Example mentioned:
- Shopify has **8,000 apps** in its ecosystem.

Why this matters:
- A large app ecosystem can create switching costs and make the platform more valuable over time (a potential competitive advantage).

#### D) Competitive Positioning
Shopify claims a strong market position:
- #2 in **US retail e-commerce** (behind Amazon)
- Larger than Walmart in that specific context (as presented)

Why this matters:
- The speaker prefers companies that are **#1 or #2** in their market.
- Reasoning:
  - Leaders have pricing power, brand strength, distribution, and scale.
  - “Chasers” often compete by lowering prices, which can hurt margins.

#### E) Growth Evidence (Numbers Given)
The presentation shows growth rates clearly:
- **57% revenue growth** (2021 vs 2020)
- **41% growth** (Q4 vs Q4 year-over-year)

The speaker also notes a visible growth jump during **COVID**, when e-commerce demand surged.
Why this matters:
- You’re checking whether the growth story is supported by actual trends and metrics.

---

concepts.title: 7) The “Stop Early” Rule: When Fundamentals Research Ends Quickly
concepts.body:
A major actionable idea: you can reject an investment *before* doing heavy financial analysis.

Stop (or pause) if:
- You don’t understand the business model.
- You can’t explain how the company will grow over the next 3–5 years.
- The growth charts/metrics don’t show meaningful expansion.
- The company is not a leader and has no clear catalyst (e.g., a pipeline breakthrough, patent approval, major product shift).

This saves time and reduces the risk of “analysis paralysis” on companies you shouldn’t own anyway.

---

concepts.title: 8) Why Business Understanding Protects You From “Numbers Games”
concepts.body:
The speaker warns:
- Companies can “tell stories” and still be weak underneath.
- There are many historical cases across markets (US, Israel, Europe, Japan) where:
  - The narrative looked great,
  - But the underlying business was not real or not sustainable.

Core principle:
- If you don’t understand the business, you can be misled by selective metrics, accounting presentation, or marketing.

So the order is:
1) Understand the business and market reality  
2) Then validate with financial statements and spreadsheets

---

concepts.title: 9) Concentration and Knowing What You Own (Charlie Munger Reference)
concepts.body:
The speaker references Charlie Munger’s idea:
- Don’t own too many companies if you can’t truly understand them.
- Example claim: “No need to invest in more than ~4 companies” (as a philosophy of deep understanding).

Whether or not you follow the exact number, the lesson is:
- **Depth beats breadth** for fundamental investing.
- You should be able to explain your holdings clearly.

---

takeaways.text: Is this a **3–5 year investment** or a short-term trade?
Google: **“[Company] IR”**
Open the **Investor Relations** site.
Identify:
What the company sells
Who the customers are
How the company makes money
What drives growth
Is the company **#1 or #2** in its market?
If not: is there a credible catalyst that could change that?
Revenue growth rates (e.g., Shopify showed **57% YoY**)
Quarterly YoY comparisons (e.g., **41% Q4 YoY**)
Note one-time boosts (e.g., COVID surge) vs sustainable trends.
If you can’t explain the business simply, or you don’t see a believable growth path—stop and move on.
After you understand the story, validate it with:
Income statement, balance sheet, cash flow (covered in the next part per the speaker)

glossary.definition: Business and financial analysis used to evaluate a company as a long-term investment.
A company’s official investor information hub (presentations, earnings releases, filings).
A slide deck explaining the business model, market, strategy, and key metrics.
Total shares issued; determines how ownership is divided among shareholders.
Smaller companies that often buy subscription tools/services.
Comparing a metric to the same period one year earlier (e.g., Q4 this year vs Q4 last year).
Buying/selling online; in this context, the infrastructure enabling online stores and payments.

title: Fundamental Valuation (Part 2): Using EPS, Growth, and P/E to Estimate a “Fair Buy Price” for Long-Term Investing
learn_items: How to estimate whether a **profitable** stock looks **cheap or expensive** using a simple spreadsheet model.
How **EPS**, expected **5-year growth**, **P/E**, your required **annual return**, and a **margin of safety** interact to produce a “fair price today.”
Why you must validate inputs (especially growth and EPS) by understanding the business and reading **Investor Relations** materials.
How to use the model to compare a **growth stock** (Shopify) vs. a **slower-growth value stock** (Intel).

concepts.title: 1) Long-term investing vs. short-term trading (why fundamentals matter)
concepts.body:
- The lesson is built for **long-term investing** (roughly **3–5 years**).
- If your plan is to hold for **months** and sell after a quick rise, deep fundamental valuation is less useful:
  - A stock can be “expensive” by fundamentals and still rise another **50–100%** in the short run.
- For long-term investing, you’re effectively “buying a business,” so you care about:
  - Earnings power
  - Growth durability
  - Whether today’s price offers a good expected return

---

concepts.title: 2) The spreadsheet valuation model (what it’s based on)
concepts.body:
The model is an **EPS-driven** valuation approach. It uses:
- **Expected growth rate (next 5 years)**  
- Current **EPS (Earnings Per Share)**
- A chosen **P/E multiple**
- Your required **annual return**
- Optional **Margin of Safety**

Important limitation:
- This model assumes the company is **profitable** (has meaningful EPS).  
  - If a company has no earnings (or negative earnings), EPS-based valuation won’t work well.

---

concepts.title: 3) Step-by-step inputs (the “recipe”)
concepts.body:
The transcript describes a clear sequence of inputs:

#### Step 1 — Identify the stock
- Enter the **ticker** (for your own tracking).

#### Step 2 — Find expected growth (next 5 years)
- Use sources like **Yahoo Finance → Analysis** tab:
  - Look for “Next 5 years (per annum)” growth estimate.
- Example:
  - **Shopify (SHOP): ~38.14%** expected annual growth (per analysts)
  - **Intel (INTC): ~3.38%** expected annual growth (per analysts)

Key insight:
- Analyst estimates are a starting point—not truth. You may adjust them if you understand the business well.

#### Step 3 — Enter EPS (and watch for one-time distortions)
- **EPS (Earnings Per Share)** = company earnings divided by shares outstanding.
- The instructor warns: EPS can be distorted by **one-time events** (e.g., a special gain from an acquisition).
- Best practice:
  - If EPS looks “weird” (e.g., suddenly extremely high), go to the company’s **Investor Relations (IR)** site and read the financial highlights.
- Shopify example:
  - A strange EPS number appeared due to a one-time effect.
  - From IR materials, the instructor uses **EPS = 6.41** instead.

Why this matters:
- A valuation model is only as good as its inputs. Garbage in → garbage out.

#### Step 4 — Choose a P/E multiple
- **P/E (Price-to-Earnings)** is the multiple the market pays for $1 of earnings.
- The instructor pulls P/E from a site like **Seeking Alpha** (Trailing 12 Months).
- Shopify example:
  - **P/E ≈ 28.82**
- Interpretation is contextual:
  - Some investors say **P/E > 20** is “expensive”
  - Others say for fast growers, **< 50** can still be “reasonable”
- Key point: **Growth stocks** can justify higher P/E than slow-growth stocks.

#### Step 5 — Set your required annual return
- You must decide what return you demand from the investment.
- Benchmark:
  - **S&P 500** historically averages about **8–10%** per year (as referenced in the video).
- Example:
  - The instructor uses **15%** required annual return for Shopify in the model.

Important compounding note:
- If you target 15% per year for 5 years, total return is **not** 15% × 5.
- Returns compound: each year’s gain builds on the previous year’s higher base.

#### Step 6 — Add a Margin of Safety (optional but recommended)
- **Margin of Safety** = a buffer to protect you if your assumptions are too optimistic.
- Example used: **10%**
- Conceptually:
  - If your growth estimate, EPS quality, or P/E assumption is wrong, the margin of safety reduces the chance you overpay.

---

concepts.title: 4) What the model outputs (how to interpret it)
concepts.body:
The spreadsheet produces an estimate of:
- A **“fair price today”** based on:
  - projected earnings growth,
  - chosen P/E,
  - and your required return.
- It can also show:
  - implied future price targets (end of year / end of period),
  - and expected multi-year return if assumptions hold.

Core use:
- Compare **current market price** vs. **model fair price**
  - If market price is far above fair price → potentially **expensive**
  - If market price is below fair price → potentially **undervalued**

---

concepts.title: 5) Example 1 — Shopify (SHOP): high growth, still can be overpriced
concepts.body:
Inputs used (from the transcript):
- Expected 5-year growth: **38.14%**
- EPS: **6.41** (from IR, adjusted for one-time effects)
- P/E: **~28.82**
- Required return: **15%**
- Margin of safety: **10%**

Model result:
- Estimated fair price “today”: **~$403.99**
- Market price at the time: **~$664**
- Interpretation:
  - Under these assumptions, Shopify looked **expensive**.

Then the instructor demonstrates “playing with assumptions”:
- If you assume the market will pay a higher P/E (e.g., **35** instead of ~28):
  - The fair price estimate rises (because you’re willing to pay more per $1 of earnings).
- If you assume even higher growth (e.g., **45%** instead of 38%):
  - The fair price rises, but the instructor notes this may be risky.
- This is the model’s real value:
  - It shows **which assumption** must be true to justify today’s price.

Practical decision framing:
- If the model suggests fair value is $645 by end of year and price is $664:
  - You might “pay up” if you believe the market will rise soon.
  - Or you might wait for a better entry.

---

concepts.title: 6) Inflation and the “cash on the sidelines” trade-off
concepts.body:
A useful real-world point raised:
- If inflation is running around **7–10%**, holding cash can lose purchasing power.
- So waiting for a perfect entry price has an opportunity cost:
  - Example logic: If you wait for $645 instead of buying at $664, the difference is ~$19.
  - But if you sit in cash during high inflation, you may lose more than that in real terms.

This is not a rule to buy anything at any price—just a reminder to weigh:
- valuation vs. timing vs. inflation vs. opportunity cost.

---

concepts.title: 7) Example 2 — Intel (INTC): low growth assumptions drive “fair value” down
concepts.body:
Initial inputs (from transcript):
- Analyst expected 5-year growth: **3.38%**
- EPS: **~4.89**
- P/E: **~9.79**
- Required return: **12%**
- Margin of safety: (initially none)

Model result (with low growth):
- Fair price estimate around **$32**
- Market price at the time: **~$45**
- Interpretation:
  - With only ~3.38% growth, Intel looked **expensive**.

Then the key lesson: **your business understanding changes the model**
- The instructor argues analysts may be too pessimistic because Intel had grown more historically.
- He overrides growth to **10%**, but adds **10% margin of safety** to reflect uncertainty.

Revised result (with 10% growth + margin of safety):
- Fair price estimate becomes about **$49**
- With market price ~**$45**, Intel looks **undervalued** *if* 10% growth happens.

But he adds an important caution:
- Even if the spreadsheet says “cheap,” you must believe the business can deliver.
- He personally is not fully convinced Intel can sustain 10% growth due to lost leadership.
- If growth is only **5%**, the valuation drops and Intel may become **expensive** again.
- Conclusion: the spreadsheet “absorbs” whatever you type—so you must be honest and realistic.

---

concepts.title: 8) Where to find the data (repeatable research workflow)
concepts.body:
The transcript suggests a simple workflow:
- Search the company name + **“IR”** (Investor Relations)
  - Read presentations, annual highlights, earnings materials
  - Use this to validate EPS quality and business reality
- Use **Yahoo Finance (Analysis)** for growth estimates
- Use a market data site (e.g., **Seeking Alpha**) for **P/E (TTM)**

---

takeaways.text: Use this EPS-based model only for **profitable companies** with meaningful EPS.
Always sanity-check **EPS**:
If EPS spikes due to a one-time event, use normalized/adjusted figures from **Investor Relations**.
Treat analyst **5-year growth** as a starting point:
Adjust only if you truly understand the business drivers and risks.
Choose a **required return** intentionally:
Compare to broad market expectations (e.g., **8–10%** for S&P 500).
Apply a **Margin of Safety** (e.g., **10%**) when assumptions are uncertain.
Use the model to ask: “What must be true for today’s price to be reasonable?”
If you must assume extreme growth or a very high P/E, the stock may be priced aggressively.
Remember the macro trade-off:
Waiting for a lower price has a cost, especially during high **inflation**.
Don’t let the spreadsheet replace business understanding:
The model can justify anything if you feed it optimistic inputs.

glossary.definition: Evaluating a company based on business performance and financial statements (earnings, growth, balance sheet), typically for long-term investing.
Net earnings divided by shares outstanding; a per-share measure of profitability.
Stock price divided by EPS; how much investors pay for $1 of earnings.
Financial results from the most recent 12-month period.
The company’s official portal for financial reports, presentations, earnings releases, and shareholder information.
A valuation discount/buffer to protect against estimation errors and unexpected negative outcomes.
Increase in shares outstanding (e.g., issuing new shares), which can reduce EPS and affect valuation.
The annual percentage return you demand to justify an investment.
Returns earned on both the original investment and prior gains, causing growth to accelerate over time.

title: How to Tell If Your Portfolio Is “Solid” or “Adventurous” Using Beta (β)
learn_items: What **Beta (β)** measures and how it compares a stock’s movement to the **S&P 500**
How to interpret beta values (below 1, around 1, above 1) to judge “defensive vs. volatile” behavior
How to quickly check beta on **TradingView** and use it as a portfolio-building input
Real examples of beta values for well-known stocks (Apple, Amazon, Meta/Facebook, Bank of America, MARA)

concepts.title: ### Why “portfolio risk” suddenly matters more in choppy markets
concepts.body:
- When markets are steadily rising, many investors don’t notice (or don’t care about) **volatility** because “everything goes up.”
- In a more **choppy** (up-and-down) market, the same portfolio can feel very different:
  - A “calm” portfolio moves modestly.
  - An “adventurous” portfolio can swing sharply up and down.
- The lesson’s goal: use one simple metric to get a quick sense of how “wild” a stock tends to be relative to the overall market.

---

concepts.title: ### Beta (β): the quick “behavior vs. the market” metric
concepts.body:
- **Beta (β)** is a number that describes how a stock tends to move **relative to a benchmark**, here the **S&P 500**.
- Think of the S&P 500 as “the market.” Beta tells you whether a stock typically:
  - Moves similarly to the market
  - Moves less than the market
  - Moves more than the market

#### What beta is based on (practical view)
- Beta is calculated from historical price movements:
  - Often shown over the last **12 months** (monthly comparisons), but can also be calculated over **2–4 years** depending on the tool.
- Important: beta is **not** a full company analysis (not “due diligence”).
  - It does **not** tell you whether the company is good or bad.
  - It mainly tells you how the stock’s price tends to **react compared to the S&P 500**.

---

concepts.title: ### How to interpret beta values (rules of thumb)
concepts.body:
- **β ≈ 1.0**
  - The stock tends to move roughly in line with the S&P 500.
  - Example from the video: **Apple (AAPL) β ≈ 1.0** (very close to market behavior).
- **β < 1.0**
  - The stock tends to be **less volatile** than the S&P 500 (more “solid/defensive” behavior).
  - Example from the video: **Amazon (AMZN) β < 1.0** (even more “attached” to the S&P behavior, with smaller swings).
- **β > 1.0**
  - The stock tends to be **more volatile** than the S&P 500 (more “adventurous/aggressive” behavior).
  - Examples from the video:
    - **Meta/Facebook (FB) β ≈ 1.29** (moderately more volatile than the market)
    - **Bank of America (BAC) β ≈ 1.35** (moderately more volatile than the market)
- **Very high β (e.g., 4+)**
  - The stock can swing dramatically compared to the market.
  - Example from the video: **MARA (Marathon Digital, blockchain/crypto-related) β ≈ 4.4**
    - Interpretation given: if the S&P 500 moves ~1%, MARA might move ~4% (not perfectly 1:1, but illustrates the magnitude).

---

concepts.title: ### Using TradingView to find beta (practical tool workflow)
concepts.body:
- The instructor uses **TradingView (tradingview.com)**:
  - Free version is enough to start (paid plans add more indicators/features).
- Where beta appears:
  - On the stock’s page, scroll down to the **financial/statistics** area (where you also see items like **market cap** and other fundamentals).
  - Beta is shown as a single number (often labeled for a specific period like **1Y / 12 months**).

---

concepts.title: ### How to use beta in portfolio construction (the real “lesson”)
concepts.body:
Beta helps you avoid building a portfolio that is accidentally too extreme.

- If your portfolio is full of high-beta stocks:
  - You may experience large daily/weekly swings.
  - This can be emotionally difficult and may cause poor decisions (panic selling, chasing).
- If you want a more “solid” portfolio:
  - Tilt toward **β near 1 or below 1**
  - Be cautious with multiple **high-beta** names in the same portfolio (especially in the same theme like crypto/blockchain)

#### What beta is good for
- A fast “sanity check”:
  - “Does this stock fit my risk tolerance?”
  - “Why does this stock feel so jumpy compared to my other holdings?”
- Comparing candidates within a theme:
  - If you’re considering a sector (e.g., blockchain), beta helps you understand how violently a specific stock tends to move versus the market.

#### What beta is NOT good for (limitations)
- Beta does not tell you:
  - Whether the business is financially strong
  - Whether the stock is undervalued/overvalued
  - Whether management is good
- Beta is one metric among many; you still need broader analysis before investing.

---

takeaways.text: Check **Beta (β)** for every stock you consider, using the **S&P 500** as your reference point.
Use these quick interpretation rules:
**β < 1** → typically less volatile than the market (more “solid”)
**β ≈ 1** → similar volatility to the market
**β > 1** → more volatile than the market (more “adventurous”)
**β ~ 4** → extremely volatile (expect big swings)
Use beta to balance your portfolio:
Avoid stacking too many high-beta stocks unless you intentionally want high risk.
Remember: beta is a **behavior metric**, not a “quality” metric—combine it with other financial and business analysis.

glossary.definition: A measure of how much a stock tends to move relative to a benchmark (here, the S&P 500).
A major U.S. stock index often used as a proxy for “the market.”
How much a stock’s price fluctuates up and down over time.
The total market value of a company’s shares (mentioned as part of the data shown on TradingView).
A full research process on a company/stock; beta alone is not due diligence.

title: Value Investing Basics: 5–7 Key Metrics to Judge Whether a Stock Is “Cheap” or “Expensive”
learn_items: How a **value investor** thinks about buying a business “cheap” based on financial statements (not charts).
The core metrics used to screen for value: **P/E**, **revenue growth**, **profit growth**, **profit margin**, **assets vs. liabilities**, **free cash flow**, and **shares outstanding**.
How to quickly pull these numbers from tools like TradingView and turn them into a simple checklist.
Why a stock can be a “great company” but still a “bad price” (e.g., very high P/E like Tesla).

concepts.title: 1) Value Investing Mindset: You’re Buying a Business, Not a Ticker
concepts.body:
A **value investor** focuses on:
- The company’s **financial performance** (financial statements), not primarily on candles, trends, or chart patterns.
- Buying a company’s shares only when the **price is attractive relative to the business results**.

Core question:
- “If I invest my money here today, can I earn a better return elsewhere with similar risk?”
  - If yes → don’t buy.
  - If no → it may be a good value investment.

#### Simple “business earnings” example (from the video)
Think of a company like a cash-producing asset (similar to a rental apartment):
- Company XYZ earns **$10 per year** for 4 years → total **$40** earned over 4 years.
- If you could buy the whole company for:
  - **$10** → you’d likely buy (pay 10, receive 40 over time).
  - **$20** → still attractive (pay 20, receive 40).
  - **$30** → maybe (depends on alternatives).
  - **$40** → likely not attractive (no “bargain” left).

Now compare a **growing** company:
- Earnings: **$10 → $20 → $30 → $40** (total **$100** over 4 years).
- You’d rationally be willing to pay more—but there is still a point where the price becomes “too expensive.”

This is the bridge to valuation ratios like **P/E**.

---

concepts.title: 2) The “Value Investor” Metrics Checklist (as taught in the video)
concepts.body:
The video effectively builds a screening table. These are the key parameters:

#### A) **P/E (Price-to-Earnings)**
- **Definition:** Stock price divided by earnings (often per share).
- In the video’s rule-of-thumb:
  - **P/E < 20** → considered potentially attractive for value investors.
- Important nuance from the instructor:
  - He personally likes this metric less because it can be influenced by external factors and accounting effects—but it’s widely used.

**Interpretation used in the video:**
- A very high P/E can imply it would take many years of earnings to “earn back” your purchase price (simplified intuition).

**Example: Tesla**
- Tesla P/E shown: **~649.8**
- The instructor’s simplified interpretation:
  - “It could take ~650 years to get your money back” (in theory).
- Value investor reaction:
  - Often an immediate “no” based on this criterion alone (even if the company is growing fast).

**Example: Amazon**
- Amazon P/E shown: **~70**
- Not “value” by the <20 rule, but the instructor shows you should still examine the rest of the business quality and growth.

---

#### B) **Revenue Growth** over 3–5 years
- **Definition:** Whether **revenue** (sales) is increasing over time.
- Why it matters:
  - Rising revenue suggests demand, scale, and business expansion.

**Oracle example (TradingView numbers shown):**
- 2017 revenue: **$39.38B**
- 2020 revenue: **$40.48B**
- Conclusion: revenue grew → checkbox passes.

**Amazon example:**
- Revenue: **$177B → $386B** (about **2.5x** in 4 years)
- Strong growth signal.

---

#### C) **Net Income (Profit) Growth** over 3–5 years
- **Definition:** Whether **net income** (bottom-line profit) is increasing.
- Why it matters:
  - Growth in profit is often more important than revenue growth alone (revenue can rise while profits stagnate).

**Oracle example:**
- 2017 net income: **$3.59B**
- 2020 net income: **$13.7B**
- Conclusion: profits increased significantly → checkbox passes.

**Amazon example:**
- Net income: **$3B → $21B** (about **7x**)
- Very strong profit growth.

---

#### D) **Profit Margin**
- **Definition:** How much profit the company keeps from each dollar of revenue.
  - In simple terms: “For every $1 that comes in, how much is left as profit?”
- Why it matters:
  - Indicates pricing power, cost control, and business efficiency.
- The instructor frames it as:
  - Check whether the “gap” between revenue and profit is improving.

(He mentions it’s harder to view quickly in the chart view, but it’s part of the checklist.)

---

#### E) **Assets vs. Liabilities** (Balance Sheet Strength)
- **Definition:**
  - **Assets** = what the company owns/controls (cash, receivables, equipment, etc.)
  - **Liabilities** = what the company owes (debt, obligations)
- The instructor’s practical test:
  - “Could the company pay off all its obligations if it had to?”
- Simple rule used:
  - If **Total Assets > Total Liabilities**, that’s a positive sign (financial strength).

**Oracle example:**
- Total assets (blue bar) higher than total liabilities (green bar) → passes.

**Amazon example:**
- Assets vs liabilities shown growing:
  - Assets: **131 → 321**
  - Liabilities: **103 → 227**
- Still assets > liabilities → strong financial position (even though liabilities also grew).

---

#### F) **Free Cash Flow (FCF)**
- **Definition:** Cash the business generates that is “free” after operating needs and investments (simplified).
- Why the instructor emphasizes it:
  - **Profit** can be influenced by accounting choices (“financial games,” as he calls it).
  - **Cash** is harder to fake: “refresh the bank account—cash is cash” (his simplified intuition).

**Oracle example:**
- Oracle free cash flow shown around **$13.75B** (annual figure referenced).
- Conclusion: strong cash generation → passes.

---

#### G) **Shares Outstanding** (and whether it’s shrinking)
- **Definition:** The number of shares the company has issued and that are held by investors.
- Why value investors like a declining share count:
  - If the company buys back shares, each remaining share represents a **larger ownership slice**.

**Pizza analogy (from the video):**
- A pizza cut into **16 slices** vs **8 slices**:
  - Fewer slices → each slice is bigger.
- Same with shares:
  - Fewer shares outstanding → your “slice” of the company can grow.

**Oracle example:**
- Shares outstanding decreased:
  - About **4.2B → 3.02B**
- Conclusion: share count shrinking → positive (buybacks).

---

concepts.title: 3) How to Use TradingView to Pull These Metrics Quickly
concepts.body:
The instructor demonstrates a practical workflow:
- Open the stock in **TradingView**
- Go to **Financials**
  - **Income Statement**: revenue, net income, margins, EPS
  - **Balance Sheet**: assets vs liabilities, shares outstanding
  - **Cash Flow**: free cash flow

Goal:
- Not to become an accountant immediately.
- Use a **simple filter** to decide:
  - “Is this worth deeper research, or do I skip it?”

---

concepts.title: 4) Value Investing vs Technical Analysis: “Good Company” vs “Good Entry”
concepts.body:
The video contrasts two approaches:

#### Value investor
- Decides if the company is fundamentally attractive based on the checklist.
- Then asks: “Do I buy now, tomorrow, or after a better price?”

#### Technical investor
- Looks at the **chart** and measures returns and timing.
- Example shown with Oracle:
  - Over ~5 years: about **+123%**
  - But a large part of the move happened recently:
    - Since around **Dec 2020**, Oracle rose about **+50%** (in a shorter period).
- Practical implication:
  - Even if fundamentals are good, after a sharp run-up a value investor might wait for a **correction** (a pullback) before entering.

---

concepts.title: 5) What the Examples Teach (Oracle vs Tesla vs Amazon)
concepts.body:
Use these as mental models:

#### Oracle (value-friendly example in the video)
- P/E: **~19.83** (passes the <20 rule)
- Revenue: modest growth
- Net income: strong growth
- Assets > liabilities
- Strong free cash flow
- Shares outstanding decreasing
- Also pays a **dividend** (the instructor notes value investors often like dividends)

**Lesson:** A mature, stable company can check many “value” boxes.

#### Tesla (extreme growth valuation example)
- P/E: **~649.8**
- **Lesson:** A company can be exciting, but the valuation can be so high that a strict value investor rejects it immediately.

#### Amazon (expensive but strong fundamentals)
- P/E: **~70** (fails the strict <20 rule)
- But:
  - Revenue and profits grew dramatically
  - Strong balance sheet (assets > liabilities)
- **Lesson:** Some investors may accept a higher P/E if growth and business strength justify it—but it becomes a judgment call.

---

takeaways.text: Rule-of-thumb from the video: **P/E < 20** (not perfect, but a common filter).
Prefer consistent growth, not shrinking sales.
Prefer rising profitability over time.
Ask: “Is the company keeping more profit per $1 of revenue over time?”
Check **Total Assets > Total Liabilities**.
Prefer strong, consistent **FCF** generation.
Prefer stable or declining share count (buybacks), not heavy dilution.
Even if fundamentals are strong, consider whether the stock has recently surged and whether waiting for a **correction** improves your entry.

glossary.definition: An investor who seeks stocks trading below their perceived intrinsic value, focusing on financial statements and business fundamentals.
Price relative to earnings; a common valuation multiple.
Total sales/income from operations.
Profit after all expenses, interest, and taxes.
Profit as a percentage of revenue (how much profit per $1 of sales).
What a company owns/controls.
What a company owes.
Cash generated that’s available after necessary spending; often used to judge financial quality.
Total shares currently issued; decreases often indicate buybacks.
Cash payment to shareholders; often favored by value-oriented investors.
A meaningful pullback in price after a rise, sometimes used as a better entry point.

title: Using Koyfin to Do Fundamental Stock Analysis (and Why It’s Not a Technical-Trading Tool)
learn_items: How **Koyfin** works as a practical alternative to expensive terminals (e.g., Bloomberg) for **fundamental analysis** and market dashboards.
How to navigate Koyfin’s main areas: **market dashboards**, **watchlists**, **screeners**, **news**, and **security analysis**.
How to analyze a stock (example: **AAPL**) using **valuation**, **capital structure**, **analyst estimates**, and **price targets**—and how to spot mismatches between price and expectations.

concepts.title: 1) Why Koyfin (and what it’s for)
concepts.body:
- The speaker looked for a **Bloomberg alternative** (Bloomberg mentioned as ~$2,500/month).
- Conclusion (as of Oct 2022): **Koyfin** felt like the most convenient option among alternatives.
- Core positioning:
  - **Not ideal for pure technical trading** (candles, chart patterns, day-trading workflows).
  - Strong for **fundamental + market data visualization**, especially when you want to connect:
    - **Price action** (the stock chart)
    - With **financial metrics over time** (revenue, margins, EPS, free cash flow, etc.)
  - Key value: ability to **cross-reference** market data with fundamentals and estimates in one place.

concepts.title: 2) Koyfin layout: what you can do from the home dashboard
concepts.body:
Koyfin’s home screen is a “market cockpit” you can customize.

Key modules mentioned:
- **Indices view** (default): major indices + **VIX** (volatility index).
  - You can change time ranges (5 days, 1 month, 3 months, etc.).
  - You can choose what appears in the center panel (e.g., sectors instead of indices).
- **Fixed Income** area:
    - Bond-related data and yields by country (useful for macro context).
- **News panel**:
    - “Market news” updates automatically (pulled from Twitter and other sources).
  - Organized into tabs like:
    - **Top News**
    - **Global Market**
    - **Corporate Events**
    - **Industries**
  - Clicking an item opens the relevant story (sometimes with a short delay).

**Practical use:** Build a daily routine dashboard:
- Indices + VIX
- Sector performance
- Bond yields
- Key news headlines
- Movers (gainers/losers)

concepts.title: 3) “Market Movers”: reading breadth and identifying leaders/laggards
concepts.body:
The **Market Movers** section shows what’s moving the most, and can be filtered.

What you can do:
- Switch universes (example given: from S&P 500 to **Nasdaq 100**).
- See distribution of:
  - **Gainers vs. losers** (market breadth snapshot)
  - Example: the speaker notes a ratio like “more decliners than advancers” (he cites a “1 to …” relationship favoring decliners at that moment).
- Sort by:
  - **Volume** (trading activity)
  - % move
  - Index weight (how important it is inside the index)

**Actionable insight:** Use this to quickly answer:
- “Is today broad strength or just a few names?”
- “Which stocks are dragging the index?”
- “Where is unusual volume showing up?”

concepts.title: 4) Watchlists: building lists with your own columns and logic
concepts.body:
Koyfin allows multiple **watchlists**, each with its own:
- Set of tickers
- Columns/metrics
- Sorting rules

Example watchlist strategy from the video:
- A list of **technology stocks with high growth**
- Rule used: **Revenue growth > 20%** (speaker mentions “over 20%” and references a forward-looking window like “over the next two years”)
  - This is essentially a simple **factor-based filter**:
  - Sector = Technology
  - Growth factor = high expected revenue growth

**Why this matters:**
- Instead of analyzing one stock at a time, you can:
  - Define a rule
  - Generate a universe
  - Then research the best candidates

concepts.title: 5) Screener: systematic filtering (“find me all stocks that match X”)
concepts.body:
The **Screener** is described as a place for “crossing searches” (combining filters).

Core idea:
- You create filtered lists (screen results), then:
  - Save them
  - Load them into dashboards and reports later

**Practical workflow:**
1. Screen for a theme (e.g., high growth tech, profitable small caps, low leverage, etc.)
2. Save as a list
3. Track it in a dashboard with charts + news + key fundamentals

concepts.title: 6) Dashboards: your personal “research workspace”
concepts.body:
You can create custom **dashboards** for different tasks.

Examples shown:
- A “review stocks” dashboard:
  - Left: a list of stocks
  - When you click a stock, it shows:
    - A chart
    - Relevant news
- A side-by-side dashboard:
  - **Technical** view next to **fundamental** view
  - Fundamental items mentioned:
    - **Revenue**
    - **EPS (Earnings Per Share)**
    - **Free Cash Flow**
    - **Sales vs. earnings results vs. estimates** (actual vs forecast comparisons)

**Actionable insight:** Build dashboards by purpose:
- “Daily market check”
- “Earnings season watch”
- “Long-term compounders”
- “High growth candidates”

concepts.title: 7) Market dashboard tools: heatmaps, calendars, multi-charts
concepts.body:
Tools highlighted:
- **Lots of Charts**: a grid (example: 9 charts) to compare multiple tickers quickly.
  - Useful for scanning your portfolio or a watchlist over a chosen period (e.g., last quarter).
- **Market Sector / Heatmap-style view**:
  - Visual grouping by metrics like **market cap** or other chosen variables.
- **Earnings Calendar**:
  - Shows which companies report each day
  - Includes **expectations** (what the market/analysts anticipate)
  - Helps you decide whether to “take cover” (reduce risk) around earnings.

concepts.title: 8) Advanced Search: searching inside transcripts (macro-to-micro research)
concepts.body:
A powerful feature mentioned: search within **earnings call transcripts**.

Example:
- Search the word **“inflation”**
  - Koyfin returns a list of earnings transcripts where that term appears

**How to use this:**
- Identify which sectors/companies are most impacted by a theme (inflation, recession, AI, supply chain, etc.)
- Quickly find management commentary across many companies

concepts.title: 9) Security Analysis (single-stock deep dive) — Example: Apple (AAPL)
concepts.body:
The speaker opens **AAPL** and explores the “Snapshot” area.

#### 9.1 Snapshot sections you can access
Koyfin provides tabs/sections like:
- **Overview**
- **Description**
- **Dividend**
- **ETF exposure**
- **Mutual fund exposure**
- **Price target**
- **Estimates overview**
- **Estimate trends**
- **Earnings history** (mentioned as available)

#### 9.2 Key fundamental metrics shown (capital structure + valuation)
In the AAPL snapshot, the speaker points to:
- **Market Cap** (equity value)
- **Debt**
- **Cash**
- **Enterprise Value (EV)**:
  - Defined as: **EV = Market Cap + Debt − Cash**
  - Useful because it reflects the value of the operating business regardless of capital structure.
- Valuation multiples:
  - **P/E (TTM)** = price-to-earnings over the **last 12 months**
  - **Forward P/E (Next 12 months)** = based on expected earnings
  - **Price-to-Book**
  - **EV/EBITDA**

**Actionable insight:** Don’t rely on one multiple:
- Compare **P/E** with **EV/EBITDA** (especially when debt/cash levels are meaningful).
- Compare **TTM vs forward** to see if the market expects growth or decline.

#### 9.3 Dividend details
Dividend panel includes:
- Whether the company pays a dividend
- **Dividend yield**
- Dividend amount
- Last dividend date

**Use case:** For income investors, quickly confirm:
- Is the yield meaningful?
- Is it consistent?
- How does it compare to peers?

#### 9.4 ETF exposure: “If I don’t want to buy Apple directly…”
Koyfin shows which **ETFs** hold the stock and how much.

Why it matters:
- If you prefer diversified exposure, you can choose an ETF that:
  - Holds the stock at a higher weight
  - Matches your strategy (tech-heavy, broad market, dividend, etc.)
- You can sort ETF holders by:
  - % holding / weight
  - Other fund metrics (as available)

#### 9.5 Analyst price targets and ratings (AAPL example)
Koyfin aggregates analyst coverage:
- Example numbers mentioned for Apple:
  - **44 analysts** covering the stock
  - Ratings breakdown includes categories like:
    - **Strong Buy**
    - **Buy**
    - **Hold**
    - (and below Hold = sell categories)
- Shows:
  - The **range** of price targets (low / average / high)
  - Whether the **average target** is rising or falling over time
  - Visual comparison: current price vs target range

**How to use this correctly:**
- Analyst targets are not “truth,” but they are a sentiment/expectations gauge.
- Useful questions:
  - Is the stock trading **above** the average target (possibly overheated)?
  - Is it **below** targets (market pessimism vs analyst optimism)?
  - Are targets being revised up/down recently?

concepts.title: 10) Estimates vs price: spotting divergences (important insight)
concepts.body:
In **Estimates Overview**, the speaker highlights a key analytical technique:

- Black line = **stock price**
- Blue line = **analyst estimates** (example discussed: sales/revenue estimates)

Observation in the video (AAPL):
- Since January, the **stock price** was in a downtrend
- But analysts had **not reduced** their **sales estimates** meaningfully (estimates kept rising)
- Conclusion: Analysts can be wrong or slow to adjust—this mismatch is a research signal.

**Actionable interpretation checklist:**
- If price falls but estimates stay high:
  - Market may be anticipating weaker future results than analysts model
  - Or valuation/macro conditions changed (rates, risk appetite)
  - Or analysts haven’t updated yet
- Next step: verify by checking:
  - Recent guidance
  - Margin pressure
  - Demand indicators
  - Transcript commentary (use the transcript search)

concepts.title: 11) Chart settings: log scale and multi-series comparisons
concepts.body:
A small but useful technique:
- Right-click chart axis/settings to change parameters such as:
  - **Log scale** (useful for long-term growth comparisons)
  - Aligning/combining series (e.g., price vs estimates)

takeaways.text: Use **Koyfin primarily for fundamental research**, dashboards, screening, and estimates—not as your main day-trading charting platform.
Build a workflow:
When analyzing a stock:
Check **Market Cap, Debt, Cash, EV**
Compare **TTM P/E vs Forward P/E**
Review **EV/EBITDA** for capital-structure-neutral valuation
Look at **analyst target range** and whether targets are trending up/down
Watch for **divergence**:
If **price declines** while **estimates rise**, treat it as a prompt to investigate what the market is worried about.

glossary.definition: A volatility index often called the market’s “fear gauge.”
Company value based on share price × shares outstanding.
Balance sheet items used to understand leverage and liquidity.
**Market Cap + Debt − Cash**; approximates the value of the operating business.
Price divided by earnings over the last 12 months.
Price divided by expected earnings over the next 12 months.
Valuation multiple comparing enterprise value to operating earnings proxy (EBITDA).
Annual dividend per share ÷ share price.
Analyst’s estimated fair value price for a stock.
Forecasts for revenue, EPS, etc., often aggregated as an average.
Schedule of upcoming earnings reports and expectations.

title: Value Investing Fundamentals: A Practical Checklist to Decide if a Company Is Worth Buying
learn_items: How to judge whether a stock looks **cheap or expensive** using a simple value-investing framework
The core **fundamental metrics** to check: valuation, growth, profitability, debt, cash generation, and share count
Concrete **benchmarks** (e.g., P/E < 20, revenue/net income growth targets) and how to adapt them by company type
Why **share buybacks** and **free cash flow** matter to long-term investors

concepts.title: 1) The core idea: “Even fundamentals investors want to buy cheap”
concepts.body:
- The lesson starts from a key principle: regardless of whether you do **technical analysis** or **fundamental analysis**, you generally want to **buy at a good price**.
- A checklist helps you:
  - Evaluate companies consistently
  - Avoid emotional decisions
  - Know exactly **what you’re compromising on** when a stock doesn’t meet your ideal criteria

---

concepts.title: 2) Where to find the data (tooling)
concepts.body:
- The speaker uses **TradingView**:
  - Open a stock (example mentioned: Apple)
  - Go to **Financials** to see the metrics discussed
- The same data should exist on other finance sites; TradingView is just the interface used in the video.

---

concepts.title: 3) Valuation: P/E (Price-to-Earnings) as the first filter
concepts.body:
- **P/E ratio (Price-to-Earnings)** definition:
  - **P/E = Share Price ÷ Earnings Per Share (EPS)**
  - Interprets “how much you pay for $1 of earnings”
- Benchmark given:
  - **Target: P/E < 20** (the “iron rule” in this checklist)
- Important nuance:
  - The speaker notes the market is currently priced “somewhat expensive,” so **finding P/E < 20 may be hard**
  - If you insist on <20, you may find:
    - Fewer companies
    - Or companies that are “cheap for a reason”
- Practical takeaway:
  - If you accept **P/E 30–40**, that’s a **deliberate compromise**—not automatically wrong, but you should be aware you’re paying up.

---

concepts.title: 4) Business growth: Revenue growth over 3–5 years
concepts.body:
- **Revenue growth** is a key sign the business is expanding.
- The checklist timeframe:
  - Look at **3 to 5 years** of history (depending on company age and available data)
- Benchmark given:
  - **At least ~6% revenue growth** over the period (the speaker says you could choose 5%, 10%, etc.)
- How to think about the number (context matters):
  - **Mature/steady companies** (example: banks) typically won’t grow revenue at very high rates
  - **Growth companies** (example mentioned: Snowflake) are expected to grow much faster
- Actionable method:
  - Compare revenue “then vs. now” across the chosen period and compute the growth rate.
  - Adjust your required growth rate based on whether it’s a mature company or a high-growth company.

---

concepts.title: 5) Profit growth: Net income / earnings growth over 3–5 years
concepts.body:
- The checklist also requires profit growth, not just revenue growth.
- Terms used:
  - **Net income**, **earnings**, **earnings growth**
- Benchmark given:
  - **At least ~6% net income growth** (again adjustable)
- Why this matters:
  - If **revenue grows but profits shrink**, the company may be becoming **less efficient** (costs rising faster than sales).
  - If **profits grow but revenue shrinks**, it can look good short-term (cost cutting), but long-term it may signal a **shrinking business**.

---

concepts.title: 6) Profitability: Margins (how much of each dollar becomes profit)
concepts.body:
- The video emphasizes **profit margins** as a crucial quality metric.
- **Profit margin** concept:
  - For every **$100** of revenue, how much is kept as profit?
  - Similar to thinking about salary: gross income is nice, but what matters is what you keep.
- The checklist focuses on margin improvement:
  - The speaker mentions **gross margin** and also notes there are other margin types (like operating margin).
- Benchmark given (for value-style analysis):
  - Aim for **gross margin growth of ~3%** over the period (a relatively “gentle” requirement so you don’t reject too many companies)
- What you’re looking for:
  - Not only growing sales, but also improving the **profitability per dollar of sales**

---

concepts.title: 7) Balance sheet safety: Assets vs. liabilities (can the company cover its debts?)
concepts.body:
- The checklist includes a balance sheet reality check:
  - **Assets** vs. **Liabilities**
- Core question:
