**What it tries to provide**
- A large set of analytics for **retail investors**, including:
  - Stocks, options, crypto
  - Social chatter tracking (Twitter / WallStreetBets style discussion)
  - Alerts and activity monitoring

**Example: looking up Square (SQ)**
Tradytics can show:
- **Market cap**
- **P/E ratio**
- Chart with auto levels like **support/resistance**
- **Anchored VWAP**
- Analyst ratings
- **Short interest** (and whether it’s high/low)
- Options activity (including “live” style flows, depending on features)

**Warning from the creator**
- It can cause **information overload**.
- If investing isn’t your full-time job, you may not need it.
- Best suited for more advanced users who know what they’re looking for.

---

concepts.title: 9) Data quality and consistency: why he sticks to a small toolset
concepts.body:
A practical, often overlooked point:

- Different websites can show **different data** for the same company.
- If one site shows “no revenue” and another shows revenue, your investment decision could change.
- He prefers using a consistent set of tools he trusts rather than constantly switching sources.

**Actionable implication**
- Cross-check critical numbers (especially financial statement items), and be cautious when a datapoint looks wrong.

---

takeaways.text: Build a simple system:
Track holdings in **Google Sheets** (or Excel) + auto-price updates.
Use the right tool for the job:
**Google Finance** = fast snapshot + quick news
**Yahoo Finance** = deeper fundamentals + options chain + chart drawings
**TipRanks** = analyst targets + insider activity + idea discovery
**TradingView** = technical analysis + convenient financial statement viewing
**Twitter/X** = real-time sentiment and headlines (verify elsewhere)
**Tradytics** = advanced analytics (only if you can handle data volume)
Match your workflow to your style:
**Investing**: understand the business + financials + analyst/insider context
**Trading**: prioritize chart structure, levels, and indicators
Avoid “tool hopping”:
Stick to a small set of reliable sources to reduce confusion and data conflicts.
Watch for **information overload**:
More data is not always better—especially for part-time investors.

glossary.definition: The stock’s trading symbol (e.g., TSLA, AAPL, NVDA).
Company value = share price × shares outstanding.
Number of shares traded in a period (often daily).
Price levels where buying (support) or selling (resistance) tends to appear.
A line drawn on a chart to visualize the direction of price movement.
Average price over a set period (e.g., 50-day MA).
A moving average that weights recent prices more heavily.
List of available options contracts (calls/puts) by strike and expiration.
Share price divided by earnings per share; a common valuation metric.
The amount of shares sold short (often shown as a % of float).
**Volume-Weighted Average Price** calculated starting from a chosen date/event; blends price and volume into one reference level.
A stock heavily driven by social media attention and retail sentiment rather than fundamentals.

title: Earnings Season Prep: How to Find Report Dates, Read Expectations, Use History, and Estimate the Market’s Move
learn_items: How **earnings season** works (timing, what companies must report, and why stocks can move ±10–20%)
Where to find a company’s **earnings date/time**, the market’s **EPS and revenue expectations**, and the actual results
How to use a company’s **earnings history** (consistent beats vs. misses) to understand market expectations
How to estimate the **expected post-earnings move** using **options pricing** (simple, practical method)

concepts.title: 1) What “Earnings Season” Is (and why it matters)
concepts.body:
- **Earnings season** happens **4 times per year**, after each quarter ends.
- Standard quarters:
  - **Q1:** Jan–Mar  
  - **Q2:** Apr–Jun  
  - **Q3:** Jul–Sep  
  - **Q4:** Oct–Dec
- Why this particular season is “special” (Q4 2023 in the video):
  - For many **technology** companies, Q4 is often strong.
  - For **retail** companies, Q4 is often strongest due to **holiday/Christmas shopping**.
  - Companies also give **forward guidance** for the next year (here: guidance for **2024**), which can move stocks as much as the past quarter’s numbers.

**Market behavior around earnings**
- The market often becomes **more volatile**:
  - Stocks may jump **+10% to +20%** or drop **-10% to -20%** around the report.

---

concepts.title: 2) The Two Headline Numbers: EPS and Revenue
concepts.body:
When a company reports, the market focuses first on two easy-to-compare metrics:

1) **Revenue** (called “top line” / “sales”)
- The total money coming into the business.

2) **EPS (Earnings Per Share)** (profitability per share)
- Simplified logic described in the transcript:
  - Revenue minus expenses = profit
  - Profit divided by number of shares = **EPS**

**Beat vs. Miss**
- The key is not just the number itself, but the number **relative to expectations**:
  - **Beat** = actual > expected
  - **Miss** = actual < expected

---

concepts.title: 3) A Crucial Reality: Most Companies “Beat” (70–80%)
concepts.body:
The transcript gives an important rule-of-thumb:

- Historically, about **70%–80%** of companies **beat** earnings expectations.

**Why so many beats happen**
- Companies have incentives to **guide conservatively** (set lower targets) so they can beat.
- Management compensation/bonuses are often tied to hitting targets—so targets may be set to be achievable.

**Practical implication**
- A “beat” alone may not be impressive if the market already expects companies to beat.
- Sometimes the stock falls even after a beat if the beat is not “good enough.”

---

concepts.title: 4) Where to Find Earnings Dates and Official Reports (Investor Relations)
concepts.body:
#### A) The simplest method: the company’s Investor Relations page
- Google:  
  **[Company Name] + “IR”** or **“Investor Relations”**
- Example from the transcript:
  - Search: **PNC IR** → go to PNC’s Investor Relations site
  - You can find:
    - Earnings release date
    - **Earnings call** time
    - Press releases and prior quarter materials

**What you can access for free**
- The **earnings press release**
- Slides / presentation (if provided)
- The **conference call webcast** (any investor can listen)

#### B) Understand the two-part earnings event
A company typically does two separate things:

1) **Earnings release** (the numbers are published)
2) **Earnings call / conference call** (management discusses results + answers analyst questions)

**Timing detail from the transcript**
- Earnings are usually released either:
  - **Pre-market** (before the market opens) — often early morning US time
  - **After-hours** (right after the market closes) — around 4:00–5:00pm US time  
    - Example: Apple is known to report around **4:30pm** sometimes.

**Important nuance**
- The earnings call time may be later than the release.
  - Example described: results might be released pre-market, but the call could be at **1:00pm**.

---

concepts.title: 5) “Quiet Period”: Why Information Shouldn’t Leak (in theory)
concepts.body:
- About **two weeks before quarter-end** through the earnings release, companies enter a **quiet period**:
  - They generally avoid discussing performance with investors/bankers
  - The idea is to prevent selective disclosure

**Practical implication**
- You should assume you *don’t* have an informational edge going into earnings.
- Price moves are driven by expectations, positioning, and new information released at earnings.

---

concepts.title: 6) How to See Expectations and Actual Results Quickly (TradingView example)
concepts.body:
The transcript shows using **TradingView** to view:
- Earnings date
- Whether it’s **pre-market** or **after market close**
- Market expectations:
  - Expected **Revenue**
  - Expected **EPS**
- After the report:
  - Actual revenue/EPS vs. expected

**Example mentioned: JPMorgan**
- Reported **pre-market**
- The platform shows:
  - What the market expected
  - What the company delivered
- The stock showed a large red candle after missing expectations (as described)

**Key lesson**
- A miss often leads to a drop, but **not always**—because guidance and commentary can dominate.

---

concepts.title: 7) Why Guidance Can Matter More Than the Quarter (NVIDIA example)
concepts.body:
The transcript gives a classic earnings-season lesson:

- A company can have “okay” results, but if it announces:
  - huge demand,
  - strong order backlog,
  - raised guidance,
  then the stock can surge.

**Example: NVIDIA (NVDA)**
- It previously “was fine” on results, but said orders for the next 6–12 months were far above expectations and raised forecasts → stock jumped strongly.

**The opposite can happen**
- Even with decent results, if management signals weakening demand (“no one is ordering”), the stock can collapse.

---

concepts.title: 8) The “Earnings Trade” Warning: Short-Term = Gambling
concepts.body:
A strong statement from the transcript:

- For **short-term traders**, holding a stock through earnings is essentially **100% a gamble**.

**Why**
- The stock reaction depends on:
  - what the market expected,
  - how the stock has already moved into earnings,
  - guidance and narrative,
  - not just whether EPS/revenue beat.

**Key concept: “Priced in”**
- If a stock has already run up massively (example given: up **~100%** or NVDA up **200%+** in a year), the bar is extremely high.
  - If it merely meets expectations (e.g., expected 100, actual 100–101), it can still drop because the market wanted a *big* surprise.

---

concepts.title: 9) Estimating the Expected Move Using Options (simple method)
concepts.body:
The transcript begins explaining a practical technique:

- The **options market** is considered the most “sophisticated” because it constantly prices risk:
  - What if the company beats?
  - What if it misses?
- You don’t need to be an options trader to use this.

**When to measure**
- Best done **1–2 days before earnings** (or late in the week before), not a month earlier.
  - Too early = expectations aren’t fully priced yet.

**Tool shown: Yahoo Finance**
- Steps (as described):
  1) Go to **Yahoo Finance**
  2) Enter the stock ticker
  3) Open the **Options** tab
  4) Find the strike price near the current stock price (the **at-the-money** strike)
  5) Look at the **Ask** price for the call option (the transcript starts with this)

**Example started: Charles Schwab (SCHW)**
- Stock price around **$65.23**
- Look at the **$65 call**, check the **Ask** (example shown: **0.31**)

> Note: The transcript cuts off mid-explanation, but the intended lesson is: options prices can be used to infer the market’s expected move around earnings (commonly done by combining near-the-money call and put prices for the earnings expiration).

---

concepts.title: 10) Useful Sources for Earnings Calendars
concepts.body:
The transcript mentions ways to see who reports and when:
- **Company IR pages** (most reliable)
- **Trading platforms** (TradingView, broker platforms)
- Twitter account: **Earnings Whispers / eWhispers** (weekly reporting lists)

---

takeaways.text: Track every holding’s earnings date:
Google **“[Company] IR”** → confirm the official date/time and call details.
Before earnings, write down:
Expected **EPS** and expected **Revenue**
Whether the report is **pre-market** or **after-hours**
Review **earnings history**:
Does the company usually beat or miss?
Are recent quarters changing (e.g., a “consistent beater” starting to miss)?
Treat short-term earnings holds as high-risk:
A beat can still drop if expectations were higher or the stock ran up too much.
Focus on **guidance** and forward-looking commentary:
Demand, orders, backlog, and next-quarter/year outlook often drive the biggest moves.
Use options to gauge expected volatility:
Check near-the-money options **1–2 days before** earnings to see what move the market is pricing.

glossary.definition: The period when many companies report quarterly results (4 times per year).
Total income generated before expenses.
Profit per share; a key profitability metric.
Reporting above or below analyst expectations.
Management’s forecast for future quarters/year (often moves the stock more than past results).
Trading sessions before the open and after the close; common earnings release times.
Time when companies limit communications before earnings to avoid selective disclosure.
Market for calls/puts; often used to infer expected volatility around events like earnings.
The price sellers are offering (what you’d typically pay to buy an option immediately).

title: Earnings Season Survival Guide: How to Read Reports, Understand “Beat/Miss,” and Manage Risk
learn_items: What **earnings season** is, when it happens, and how reports + investor calls work
What to focus on in an earnings report: **revenue, EPS, guidance**, and key business metrics
How **beat/miss vs. expectations** works—and why “good results” can still send a stock down
How to manage risk around earnings using **expected move** (options-implied volatility) and trade planning

concepts.title: 1) Why earnings season matters (especially when markets are priced for growth)
concepts.body:
- Stock indexes like the **S&P 500** don’t rise “for no reason.” A major driver is the market’s expectation that aggregate corporate profits will grow.
- A key idea: prices can run ahead of fundamentals.
  - If the index rises because investors expect **EPS growth**, but later **earnings start falling**, the market may have “moved too fast” and becomes vulnerable to pullbacks.
- Practical implication:
  - When valuations are elevated, earnings season becomes more “dramatic” because results must justify the price level.

---

concepts.title: 2) What is earnings season?
concepts.body:
- **Earnings season** = the period each quarter when public companies must publish their **quarterly financial results**.
- Companies typically release:
  1. An **earnings release** (press release / report PDF)
  2. An **earnings call** (investor call) about ~1–1.5 hours later, including Q&A with analysts

**Example from the transcript:** JPMorgan (JPM) reporting segment revenues, expenses, and profit—an example of a readable earnings release.
---

concepts.title: 3) How often does it happen? (and why some weeks feel “packed”)
concepts.body:
- Earnings season happens **every quarter**.
- Not all companies use the exact same fiscal calendar:
  - Many follow the calendar year (Q1 ends March, Q2 ends June, etc.)
  - Others have different **fiscal years** and quarter definitions
- Reporting volume comes in “waves”:
  - Large, widely-followed companies (often S&P 500) tend to report earlier
  - Later, many smaller companies report, creating the biggest spikes in the number of reports (hundreds in a day)

**Why this matters:** during peak weeks, market-moving news is constant, and correlations/volatility can rise.

---

concepts.title: 4) Where to find earnings reports (the “IR” method)
concepts.body:
To find official earnings documents:
- Use Google (regular search) and type:  
  **“[Company Name] IR”** (IR = Investor Relations)
- Go to the company’s **Investor Relations** site
- Look for **Quarterly Earnings / Quarterly Results**
- Open the PDF / “View document” to see the full release

**Why this is useful:** you’re getting the company’s primary-source numbers, not a headline summary.

---

concepts.title: 5) When earnings are released: pre-market vs after-hours
concepts.body:
Earnings releases usually happen:
- **Pre-market** (before the market opens), or
- **After-hours** (after the market closes)

Key mechanics:
- Stocks can trade in **extended hours** (pre-market/after-hours), so you may see sharp moves outside regular trading.
- Your broker/platform may require enabling extended-hours trading.

---

concepts.title: 6) What to focus on inside an earnings report
concepts.body:
The transcript emphasizes three core areas:

#### A) **Revenue** (sales / top line)
- How much the company sold/earned in total
- Often broken down by **segments** (e.g., business lines, geographies)

#### B) **Profitability** and **EPS**
- **Net income** matters, but for stock pricing, **EPS (Earnings Per Share)** is critical.
- Why EPS matters:
  - “$4B profit” alone is incomplete—investors need to know how many shares that profit is spread across.
  - EPS standardizes profit per share and helps compare across time and companies.

#### C) **Guidance** (forward-looking expectations)
- Often the biggest driver of price reaction.
- Guidance can include:
  - Next-quarter revenue/EPS expectations
  - Full-year outlook
  - Growth rates, margins, demand signals

#### Industry-specific metrics (examples mentioned)
- Banks: **Net income**, plus other banking-specific line items (not fully detailed in transcript)
- Tech/subscription businesses: customer acquisition costs, churn/retention, user growth (the transcript references “how many customers left,” acquisition costs, etc.)

---

concepts.title: 7) “Beat” or “Miss”: what it actually means
concepts.body:
Before earnings, the market has **consensus expectations** (usually analyst estimates) for:
- **Revenue**
- **EPS**
- Sometimes other metrics

Definitions:
- **Beat** = actual results are higher than expected  
  Example from transcript: expected revenue 100, actual 102 → beat
- **Miss** = actual results are lower than expected  
  Example: expected 100, actual 98 → miss

Important nuance:
- There are beats/misses for revenue, EPS, and also for **guidance** (raising or lowering outlook).
---

concepts.title: 8) Where do expectations come from? (how analysts build estimates)
concepts.body:
Analysts typically:
- Read the earnings release and listen to the **earnings call**
- Update models based on:
  - Company commentary (growth, margins, demand)
  - Industry data and “field work”
  - Financial modeling (often Excel-based)

**Example from transcript (auto industry):**
- Estimate units sold × average selling price − cost to produce  
→ leads to revenue and profit estimates.

---

concepts.title: 9) Guidance vs analyst expectations (and “sandbagging”)
concepts.body:
Two different “forecast streams” exist:
1. **Company guidance**: what management says they expect
2. **Analyst expectations**: what analysts believe will happen

Key concept: **Sandbagging**
- When management gives conservative guidance (e.g., “we’ll grow 5%”)
- Analysts may believe true growth will be higher (e.g., 10%)
- If the company then delivers only 5%, it can be seen as a **miss vs analyst expectations**, even if it matches company guidance.

---

concepts.title: 10) The most important lesson: there is no “good earnings,” only market reaction
concepts.body:
The transcript’s core mindset shift:

- Don’t label a report as “good” or “bad” in isolation.
- The only thing that matters for price is:
  - What the market **expected**
  - What the company **delivered**
  - How guidance and narrative changed
  - How the stock **reacts**

Why “good numbers” can still drop:
- The company may have improved (e.g., profit up from 100 to 105)
- It may even beat consensus (expected 104, delivered 105)
- But peers may have grown faster, or the market was positioned for an even bigger upside surprise
- Result: stock can fall despite “beating”

**Concrete examples from the transcript**
- **Netflix**: earnings-day moves vary widely by quarter (examples given: -0.68%, -9%, +10%, +16%, etc.). Same company, different reactions.
- **JPMorgan**:
  - A quarter where it **missed** but the stock moved only about **-0.7%**
  - A quarter where it **beat** but the stock fell around **-6%**
  - A quarter with a large beat (~9.9% surprise mentioned) but the stock still dropped (~-2% mentioned)
  - Another recent report where the stock rose about **+4.4%**
  
Takeaway: earnings reaction is not mechanically tied to “beat/miss.”

---

concepts.title: 11) Risks of trading or holding through earnings
concepts.body:
Main risk: **volatility**
- Stocks can move a few percent—or **10–20%+**—very quickly.
- Negative surprises (e.g., **profit warning**) can cause sharp drops.
- Technical levels can fail:
  - Stocks can break **support** or **resistance** abruptly on earnings.

Rule-of-thumb mindset from the transcript:
- “Don’t trade earnings” = don’t treat it like a predictable setup.
- Buying the night before is essentially a coin flip:
  - ~50% chance up, ~50% chance down
  - And downside often “hurts more” psychologically and financially

---

concepts.title: 12) What to do if you already have an open trade before earnings
concepts.body:
A practical risk-management question:
- Ask: **Am I willing to absorb the downside move?** (not the upside)
  - If yes: you may choose to hold
  - If no (e.g., you’re at breakeven or small profit): consider closing/reducing before the report

This is position sizing and risk tolerance—not a universal rule.

---

concepts.title: 13) Estimating the “expected move” using the options market
concepts.body:
A very actionable tool mentioned:

- The options market implies an **expected move** for earnings (based on option prices / implied volatility).
- Traders can estimate how much the stock is expected to move **up or down** (±X%).

How it’s used:
- If the expected move is ±3.6%, the market is pricing a typical earnings reaction around that range.
- Actual move can be larger or smaller.
  - Example given: market expected JPM to move about **3.6%**, but it moved about **4.6%** (bigger-than-expected reaction).

Why it matters:
- Helps you decide whether your position size and stop-loss plan make sense.
- Helps set realistic expectations (earnings can gap beyond technical stops).

---

concepts.title: 14) How to check when a company reports earnings
concepts.body:
Methods mentioned:
- Look up the earnings date on your charting/market platform (often an “E” icon)
- Use earnings calendars (platforms, company IR site, etc.)

Example from transcript:
- Hovering over an “E” marker shows the expected report date (e.g., Netflix reporting after the close on a specific date).

---

concepts.title: 15) How AI can help (and what it cannot do)
concepts.body:
AI cannot reliably:
- Predict whether a stock will go up or down after earnings

AI can help:
- Summarize an earnings report into:
  - **Positive points**
  - **Negative points**
  - Key metrics and changes
- Practical workflow suggested:
  - Download the earnings report PDF
  - Ask a tool like ChatGPT: “Summarize this earnings report with positive and negative points”

Use case:
- Faster digestion of long reports, but you still must judge expectations vs results and the market’s reaction.

---

takeaways.text: Check **earnings date** for every stock you hold (platform “E” icon, earnings calendar, or company **IR** site).
Read the earnings release focusing on:
**Revenue**
**EPS**
**Guidance** (often the biggest mover)
Understand **beat/miss**:
Compare actual results to **consensus expectations**, not just last quarter/year.
Don’t assume “good report = stock up.”
The only truth is the **price reaction** vs expectations and positioning.
Manage earnings risk:
Decide in advance if you can tolerate a **gap down**.
Consider reducing/closing positions if you can’t absorb the downside.
Use **options-implied expected move** (±%) to size risk realistically.
Use AI to **summarize** reports, not to forecast price direction.

glossary.definition: The quarterly period when public companies report financial results.
Profit allocated per share; net income divided by shares outstanding (or weighted diluted shares).
Management’s forward-looking forecast (next quarter/year revenue, EPS, growth, margins, etc.).
The market/analyst average expectation for revenue/EPS before the report.
Actual results above (beat) or below (miss) consensus expectations.
Trading sessions outside regular market hours when many earnings are released.
The market-implied ±% move (often derived from options prices) around an event like earnings.
Technical analysis levels where price often reacts; earnings can break them abruptly.
When management gives conservative guidance that may be easier to beat later.

title: Earnings Season: Why “Good Results” Can Still Crash a Stock (and Why You Shouldn’t Gamble on Reports)
learn_items: How **earnings season** works (timing, what gets published, and why companies report).
Why “**good earnings**” doesn’t necessarily mean the stock will go up (and vice versa).
How to find official earnings materials via **Investor Relations** pages and finance platforms.
Why trading *into* earnings is closer to **gambling**, and how options markets imply expected moves.

concepts.title: 1) What earnings season really is (and why it matters)
concepts.body:
- Public companies must report results **every quarter** (roughly every 3 months).
- The earnings report serves two purposes:
  - Reports the **past** (what happened last quarter).
  - Guides the **future** (management’s expectations for the coming months/quarters).
- Key idea: **Investors are the owners** of public companies, so companies are obligated to disclose information.

concepts.title: 2) The market is forward-looking (expectations are everything)
concepts.body:
- The market typically prices stocks based on expectations **6–9 months ahead**.
- That’s why you can see markets rising even before results improve—because the market is already “pricing in” improvement.
- In the transcript’s example (as of April 12, 2024):
  - Major indices were up YTD (e.g., **S&P 500 ~+8.8%**, Nasdaq similar; Dow smaller; Russell weaker/negative).
  - Most sectors were positive YTD except **Real Estate** (mentioned as negative).
- Practical implication:
  - By earnings season, companies must **prove** they are on track to meet the optimistic story the market already priced in.
  - If they don’t, the stock can “get hit” even if the report looks fine on the surface.

concepts.title: 3) When companies release earnings (and why it’s usually not during market hours)
concepts.body:
- Earnings are typically released:
  - **Before the open** (usually up to ~7:00–8:00am US time).
  - **After the close** (often right at 4:00pm US time, sometimes slightly later).
- “Midday” earnings releases are considered **unusual** (“exceptional reports”) because they disrupt normal trading.
- After the earnings release, companies usually hold an **earnings call** about **1–1.5 hours later**:
  - CEO/CFO discuss results and outlook.
  - Analysts ask questions.
  - Retail investors can often listen via the company’s website.

concepts.title: 4) Why “don’t trade earnings” is a rational rule
concepts.body:
The speaker’s core rule: **Don’t trade (gamble) on earnings.**

Why:
- Normal daily stock movement might be ~2–4% for many stocks.
- On earnings, a stock can move **15–20%** (or more).
- That creates a temptation: “I can make 20% overnight.”
- But the same logic applies in reverse: you can also lose 20% overnight.
- For every bullish bet, there’s a bearish bet—earnings is a **two-sided coin flip** unless you truly have an edge.

concepts.title: 5) Options markets imply the “expected move” (but it’s not a promise)
concepts.body:
- The transcript references calculating the **expected move** from the **options market** (via implied volatility).
- A third-party account (“Despacito” nickname in the video) publishes the expected move so traders don’t have to do the math.
- Important:
  - The expected move is a **market-implied estimate**, not a guarantee.
  - It does **not** tell you direction—only the magnitude the market is pricing.

**Example from the transcript**
- A company (mentioned as “Rent Run”) was priced for about **±19.5%** move.
- It actually moved about **+161%** (extreme outlier).
  - The explanation given: the market wasn’t positioned for it; short sellers had to cover (**short squeeze** dynamics).
- Lesson: Outliers happen, but you should not build a strategy assuming outliers will repeat.

concepts.title: 6) “Good earnings” vs. “stock goes up”: there is no such thing as objectively “good”
concepts.body:
A central lesson:
- There is no universal “**good**” or “**bad**” earnings report.
- The only thing that matters for your P&L is: **How did the stock react?**
- Even if results beat expectations, the stock can fall if:
  - Guidance disappoints.
  - A key line item misses.
  - The stock was already priced for perfection.
  - “Whisper numbers” were higher than published consensus.

concepts.title: 7) Consensus estimates vs. whisper numbers
concepts.body:
- **Consensus estimate**: the average of analysts’ published forecasts (EPS, revenue, etc.).
- **Whisper number**: the unofficial expectation “circulating” among professionals that may be higher/lower than consensus.
- Why it matters:
  - A company can “beat consensus” and still drop if it missed the whisper number.
- This is a major reason earnings trading is hard: you often don’t know the real bar the market is using.

concepts.title: 8) Why a stock can drop after beating EPS and revenue (JPM example)
concepts.body:
The transcript walks through **JPMorgan (JPM)**:
- Reported:
  - **EPS**: 4.44 vs. 4.17 expected (beat)
  - **Revenue**: 42.5B vs. 41.6B expected (beat)
- Yet the stock was down about **-5%** that day.

Why (as explained):
- Investors/analysts look beyond headline EPS and revenue.
- They focus on many internal drivers and business lines.
- Specific item mentioned:
  - **Managed net interest income** (bank profitability from interest spread) came in **below expectations** (example numbers referenced around 1.31 vs 1.33—context suggests a miss vs expectation).
- Another key factor: **positioning and valuation**
  - JPM was up about **+15% YTD** and near/at **all-time highs**.
  - When a stock is at highs, the market expects “perfection.”
  - A small miss in an important metric can trigger selling.

concepts.title: 9) Price context matters: all-time highs vs. “stuck range”
concepts.body:
The speaker compares JPM vs. Wells Fargo (WFC):
- **WFC**: roughly flat on the day despite beating some headline numbers.
- Reason given: WFC wasn’t priced as aggressively; it was more “stuck” in a long-term area (not making new highs).
- **JPM**: at/near **all-time highs**, so expectations were higher and punishment harsher.

**Actionable insight**
- Always evaluate earnings reaction relative to:
  - **Where the stock is trading** (all-time high vs. depressed).
  - **How much it already ran** into earnings (YTD or last 3–6 months).

concepts.title: 10) How to find earnings dates and official materials (Investor Relations workflow)
concepts.body:
Two practical methods shown:

**A) Company Investor Relations page**
- Search: “[Company Name] + **Investor Relations**”
- Example shown: **Netflix Investor Relations**
  - You can find:
    - Earnings release
    - Shareholder letter / report
    - Presentation (slides)
    - Earnings call webcast link
    - Q&A materials (varies by company)

**B) Finance platforms showing earnings date**
- The transcript references using a platform (looks like TradingView-style) where an **“E” icon** marks earnings.
- Clicking it shows:
  - Earnings date
  - Whether it’s **After Hours** (night icon) or **Before Open**

concepts.title: 11) Earnings moves can be extreme (Snapchat example)
concepts.body:
The transcript uses **Snap (SNAP)** to show how violent earnings reactions can be:
- One earnings reaction: about **+58%** the next day (huge upside).
- Later earnings reactions included large drops (examples mentioned like **-39%**, **-28%**), showing instability and unpredictability.
- Lesson: Chasing “the next +58%” can lead to getting hit by the next big downside gap.

takeaways.text: **Don’t confuse company performance with stock performance.** A “beat” can still lead to a drop.
Before earnings, check:
Is the stock near an **all-time high**?
How much has it run **YTD** or in the last few months?
What is the market **expecting** (consensus + implied move)?
Use the **Investor Relations** site as your primary source:
Earnings release, slides, call webcast, and management commentary.
Treat earnings as a **high-volatility event**:
Expect moves far larger than normal daily volatility (often **15–20%** for many stocks).
Understand that markets react to:
Guidance and forward outlook
Key segment metrics (not just EPS/revenue)
**Whisper expectations**
Positioning/valuation (how “priced in” the good news already is)
If you still choose to trade earnings, do it with “event-risk” sizing:
Assume you could be wrong instantly by a large gap (down or up).

glossary.definition: The period when many public companies report quarterly results.
Quarterly disclosure including financial results (EPS, revenue) and often guidance.
Profit allocated per share; a key profitability metric.
Average of analysts’ published forecasts.
Unofficial expectation beyond published consensus.
Trading sessions outside regular market hours when earnings are often released.
Conference call/webcast where management discusses results and answers questions.
The price move implied by options **implied volatility** around an event.
Options market’s pricing of expected future volatility.
Rapid price rise caused by short sellers buying to cover positions.
For banks, income from interest earned minus interest paid; a key driver of profitability.

title: Estimating a Stock’s Expected Earnings Move Using Options (Implied Move)
learn_items: How the **options market** can be used to estimate a stock’s **expected price move** around earnings.
A simple calculation (using an **at-the-money straddle**) to estimate the **±% move** the market is pricing in.
How to pull the needed numbers from **Yahoo Finance → Options**.
How to interpret the result correctly: it estimates **magnitude**, not **direction**.

concepts.title: 1) Earnings week: the big question is “How much will it move?”
concepts.body:
During **earnings season**, stocks often make sharp moves because new information changes expectations.

- A stock can go up or down after earnings.
- What traders want to know in advance is often:
  - “Is the market expecting a small move (e.g., ±3%) or a big move (e.g., ±10%+)?”
- The video’s core idea: the market already embeds a “forecast” of the move through **options prices**.

Important limitation:
- This method does **not** tell you whether the stock will rise or fall.
- It estimates the **expected move range** (volatility) priced by the market.

---

concepts.title: 2) The “mechanism” that prices expected moves: the options market
concepts.body:
The presenter calls the options market the “big brother” that calculates expectations.

- **Call options** are commonly associated with bullish bets (profit if the stock rises).
- **Put options** are commonly associated with bearish bets (profit if the stock falls).

Key insight:
- Around earnings, options become more expensive because the market expects a bigger move.
- By combining the price of a call and a put near the current stock price, you can estimate the market’s expected move.

---

concepts.title: 3) The practical tool: Yahoo Finance Options chain
concepts.body:
Where to find the data:
- Go to **Yahoo Finance**
- Search the ticker
- Open the **Options** tab

What to select:
- Use the **expiration date closest** to the event you’re measuring (here: the nearest expiry right after earnings, often the same week).
- Look for the **strike price** closest to the current stock price:
  - This is the **at-the-money (ATM)** or very near ATM strike.

Why ATM?
- The ATM call + ATM put combination is the cleanest “market consensus” proxy for the expected move.

---

concepts.title: 4) The strategy behind the math: the ATM “straddle” as an implied move proxy
concepts.body:
The calculation is essentially the cost of an **ATM straddle**:

- **ATM Straddle Cost ≈ Call (ATM) premium + Put (ATM) premium**
- Then convert that dollar cost into a percentage of the stock price.

**Formula (as taught in the video):**
1. Find current stock price: **S**
2. Find ATM call premium: **C**
3. Find ATM put premium: **P**
4. Compute expected move (dollars): **C + P**
5. Convert to percent:  
   \[
   \text{Expected Move \%} \approx \frac{C + P}{S} \times 100
   \]
Interpretation:
- The result is approximately the market-implied move **up or down** (±X%) into that expiration.

---

concepts.title: 5) Worked Example #1: Amazon (AMZN)
concepts.body:
The presenter records on Oct 26 and uses the nearest expiration (Oct 27).

Numbers shown:
- Stock price (approx): **$118** (he notes ~118.5 but uses ~118 in the math)
- ATM strike: **118**
- Call premium: **$6.25**
- Put premium: **$3.65**
Calculation:
- Straddle cost = 6.25 + 3.65 = **$9.90**
- Expected move % ≈ 9.90 / 118 × 100 ≈ **8.3%**
Meaning:
- The options market is pricing roughly a **±8.3%** move by that expiration.

---

concepts.title: 6) Worked Example #2: Intel (INTC)
concepts.body:
Numbers shown:
- Stock price: **$32.76** (approx)
- ATM strike: **32.5**
- Call premium: **$1.28**
- Put premium: **$1.07**
Calculation:
- Straddle cost = 1.28 + 1.07 = **$2.35**
- Expected move % ≈ 2.35 / 32.7 × 100 ≈ **7.1%**
Meaning:
- The options market is pricing roughly a **±7.1%** move around earnings into that expiration.

---

concepts.title: 7) How to use this number (and how NOT to use it)
concepts.body:
**Correct use (risk planning):**
- Position sizing: if expected move is ±8%, don’t size your trade as if it’s a normal ±1–2% day.
- Stop-loss / take-profit realism: tight stops may get hit easily during earnings volatility.
- Decide whether you want exposure at all: high implied move = higher uncertainty.

**Incorrect use:**
- Treating ±X% as a guaranteed move.
- Assuming it predicts direction (up vs. down). It doesn’t.

---

takeaways.text: Use **Yahoo Finance → Options** to estimate the market’s expected earnings move.
Choose the **nearest expiration** after the earnings event.
Pick the **ATM strike** (closest to current stock price).
Add premiums: **ATM Call + ATM Put**.
Divide by stock price and multiply by 100 to get **expected move ±%**.
Use the result to prepare for **volatility and risk**, not to predict direction.

glossary.definition: A table listing option contracts by expiration and strike, with prices and other data.
Contract that benefits from stock price increases (right to buy at a strike).
Contract that benefits from stock price decreases (right to sell at a strike).
The price at which the option can be exercised.
The date the option contract expires.
Strike price closest to the current stock price.
The price paid for an option.
Buying a call and a put at the same strike/expiration; used to bet on movement (either direction).
The approximate ±% move the options market is pricing in (derived from option premiums).

title: How to Read Earnings Reports: The 4 Numbers That Move a Stock (Revenue, Earnings, Margins, Guidance)
learn_items: How **quarterly earnings season** works and what companies discuss in earnings calls
The 4 core items to check in every report: **Revenue**, **Earnings**, **Margins**, and **Guidance**
Why a stock can fall even when results “look good”: **results vs. analyst expectations**
How to quickly view these metrics in **TradingView** (Financials tab + analyst estimates)

concepts.title: 1) What “Earnings Season” Actually Is (and why it matters)
concepts.body:
- Public companies typically report results **once per quarter** (every ~3 months).
- The company announces the reporting date in advance.
- After publishing the official numbers, management (often the **CEO** and **CFO**) holds an **earnings call** (conference call / Zoom) with analysts.
- What happens on the call:
  - Management explains what happened in the quarter (challenges, wins, major actions)
  - They review key financial results
  - They provide **forward-looking guidance**
  - Analysts ask questions (this Q&A can move the stock)

**Where investors can listen:** usually on the company website under **Investor Relations (IR)**.
---

concepts.title: 2) The 4 Things to Focus On in Every Earnings Report
concepts.body:
The lesson’s main framework: you don’t need to track dozens of metrics—start with these four.

#### (1) **Revenue (Top Line)**
- **Revenue** = total sales / total money the company brought in during the period.
- Also called the **top line**.

**Key insight:** Revenue is not “good” or “bad” by itself. Markets react to revenue **relative to expectations**.
**Example from the video (expectations matter):**
- Company A reports **$50M revenue**, Company B reports **$25M revenue**
- But analysts expected:
  - Company A: **$75M** → actual **$50M** = **miss**
  - Company B: **$15M** → actual **$25M** = **beat**
- Result: even though A has higher revenue, B likely gets the better stock reaction because it **beat expectations**.

What to compare:
- **Quarter-over-quarter (QoQ)** trends
- **Year-over-year (YoY)** trends (same quarter last year)
- **Actual vs. analyst estimate** (beat/miss)

---

#### (2) **Earnings (Profit / Bottom Line)**
- **Earnings** = profit after expenses (simplified).
- Often discussed as:
  - **Net income**
  - **Earnings per share (EPS)** (explained below)

**Key insight:** Like revenue, earnings must be judged versus:
- **Analyst expectations**
- Prior quarters / prior years

**Important nuance from the lesson:**  
A company can report a loss and still be “okay” if it’s investing for growth. Losses are not automatically a disaster the way they might be in personal budgeting. What matters is:
- Is the company improving toward profitability over time?
- Did it do better than expected?

---

#### (3) **Margins (Profitability Efficiency)**
The video simplifies margins as: how much “net” you keep from each dollar of sales.

- **Operating margin / profit margin (simplified concept)**:
  - Think: “For every $1 of revenue, how much profit remains?”
- Why margins matter:
  - Two companies can have the same revenue, but the one with higher margins is typically:
    - more efficient
    - more profitable
    - generating more cash/profit per dollar of sales

**Example from the video (same revenue, different margins):**
- Both companies have **$100M revenue**
- Company 1 earns **$50M** → margin ≈ **50%**
- Company 2 earns **$25M** → margin ≈ **25%**
- Company 1 is more profitable even though revenue is identical.

What to look for:
- Are margins **rising over time** (good sign)?
- Are margins shrinking (could signal higher costs, pricing pressure, inefficiency)?

---

#### (4) **Guidance (Forward Outlook)**
- **Guidance** = management’s expectations about the future (next quarter / next year).
- Why it can dominate the stock reaction:
  - The market prices stocks based on the **future**, not just the past quarter.

**Example idea from the video (COVID / airlines):**
- During a shock (e.g., COVID), companies may say revenue is collapsing (e.g., “no one is flying”).
- Guidance might focus on cost-cutting to avoid taking on excessive debt.
- Bad guidance can hurt the stock even if the last quarter looked fine.

What to listen for in guidance:
- Expected revenue growth/decline
- Expected profitability/margins
- Cost plans (hiring, layoffs, spending)
- Demand commentary (strong/weak pipeline, churn, bookings, etc.)

---

concepts.title: 3) The Hidden Driver: **Analyst Expectations**
concepts.body:
A major theme: Wall Street reacts to the difference between:
- **Actual results** vs. **consensus estimates**

This explains the common confusion:
- “The company reported great numbers—why is the stock down?”

Possible answer:
- Numbers were good, but **not good enough** versus what analysts already priced in.

Practical implication:
- Always check whether the company **beat or missed**:
  - revenue estimates
  - earnings/EPS estimates
  - and whether guidance was above/below expectations

---

concepts.title: 4) How to Check These Metrics in TradingView (Quick Workflow)
concepts.body:
The video demonstrates using **TradingView** to view financials and estimates.

#### TradingView steps (as shown)
1. Open the stock chart (example used: **Netflix**).
2. Click the company panel and open **Financials**.
3. View metrics:
   - **Revenue** trend
   - **Earnings** trend
   - **Profit margin** trend
4. Switch views:
   - **Year-over-year** (annual comparison)
   - **Quarterly** (quarter-by-quarter)

#### Netflix example observations (from the video)
- YoY: revenue and earnings appeared to trend upward.
- Quarterly: profit margin was not perfectly steady (down in one quarter, up in another), showing why you should check multiple time frames.

#### Checking analyst estimates (earnings surprise)
- TradingView can show **analyst EPS estimates** and how often the company beat/missed.
- Example mentioned:
  - Netflix had quarters where it **missed** estimates (negative surprises)
  - In one quarter (Q1 2021 mentioned), it **beat** by about **24%** above estimate
- Lesson: if a company has a pattern of missing, another miss can hurt the stock; a surprise beat can lift it.

---

concepts.title: 5) EPS: Why It’s Mentioned So Often
concepts.body:
In the Apple example, the video references **earnings per share (EPS)**:
- **EPS** = earnings divided by the number of shares.
- Analysts often forecast EPS (e.g., “expected EPS = 1.01”).

**Apple example numbers shown:**
- A strong quarter (Q1 2021 mentioned):
  - Revenue: **$111B**
  - Net income: **~$29B**
  - Profit margin: **~25%**
  - Interpretation: for every **$100** Apple brought in, it kept about **$25** as profit (very high for a mega-cap).
- Analysts expected a future EPS around **1.01**, and the prior quarter actual was around **1.40** (showing how expectations and actuals are compared).

---

takeaways.text: **Revenue (Top Line)**
Is revenue growing **QoQ** and **YoY**?
Did revenue **beat or miss** analyst expectations?
**Earnings / EPS (Bottom Line)**
Is profitability improving over time?
Did EPS **beat or miss** estimates?
If the company is unprofitable, is the loss **shrinking** and/or better than expected?
**Margins**
Are **profit margins** trending up (efficiency improving)?
If margins fell, is there a clear reason (temporary vs. structural)?
**Guidance**
What does management expect next quarter/year?
Is guidance stronger or weaker than the market expected?
**Context**
Don’t judge results by absolute numbers alone—always compare to:
**analyst expectations**
**prior periods** (QoQ/YoY)
**Tooling**
Use TradingView → **Financials** to quickly visualize trends and check estimate surprises.

glossary.definition: The period when many public companies report quarterly results.
Total sales generated in the period.
Profit after costs/expenses (simplified).
Earnings divided by shares outstanding; a common analyst forecast metric.
Profitability as a percentage of revenue (how much profit is kept per dollar of sales).
Management’s forecast or outlook for future performance.
The market’s “expected” numbers; beats/misses often move the stock.
Section of a company’s website where earnings releases, presentations, and call links are posted.

title: Earnings Season (Q3 2022): How to Prepare, What to Watch, and Why Stocks Can Move Violently
learn_items: What **earnings season** is and why it creates concentrated volatility in stocks.
How to read an earnings report using **top line (revenue)**, **bottom line (EPS)**, **free cash flow**, and **guidance** vs. **analyst estimates**.
Where to find earnings dates and expectations (company **Investor Relations**, **TradingView “E” icon**, and **Earnings Whispers**).
Why a stock can crash even after “beating estimates” (Netflix example: guidance/wording matters).

concepts.title: 1) Why this earnings season is “dramatic”
concepts.body:
The video frames Q3 2022 earnings as unusually important because many macro forces collide at once. Investors listen closely for clues about the future, not just past results.

Key macro pressures mentioned:
- **High inflation**
- **Rising USD (strong dollar)**
- **Full employment**
- **Fed rate hikes**
- **Supply chain problems**
- **Higher fuel/energy costs**

What investors are trying to learn from earnings calls/reports:
- Has **inflation peaked**?
- Are **supply chain issues easing**?
- How much did the **strong dollar** hurt international revenue/profits?
- Are companies still **growing**, and at what pace?
- Most importantly: what do companies say about the **next quarter / next year**?

---

concepts.title: 2) What “earnings season” means (and why it’s called a “season”)
concepts.body:
- Public companies report results **once per quarter** (4 times per year).
- Because many companies report around the same few weeks, it becomes a concentrated “season.”

Quarter structure (typical calendar-year companies):
- **Q1:** Jan–Mar  
- **Q2:** Apr–Jun  
- **Q3:** Jul–Sep  
- **Q4:** Oct–Dec  

Core idea:
- When you own a stock, you are an **investor in the business**, and the quarterly report is like the company’s “report card” on execution (sales, operations, supply, marketing, etc.).

---

concepts.title: 3) The earnings “scoreboard”: actual results vs. expectations
concepts.body:
Earnings reactions are largely driven by comparison:

- Company reports:
  - **Revenue (Sales / Top line)**
    - **Profitability** such as **EPS (Earnings Per Share / Bottom line)**
    - Often also **Free Cash Flow**
    - And provides **guidance** (forward-looking expectations)
  - Market compares those numbers to:
  - **Analyst estimates** (Wall Street expectations)
  Possible outcomes:
- Beat on revenue, miss on EPS (or vice versa)
- Beat both
- Miss both
- Meet both but guide down (often negative)

Important nuance from the transcript:
- It’s not “only the numbers.” The **commentary** and **forward-looking statements** can dominate the stock move.
---

concepts.title: 4) Who are analysts and what do they do?
concepts.body:
**Analysts** are professionals (often at banks/research firms) who cover a company and publish expectations.

Example given: Nike
- Nike has **34 analysts** covering it (in the example screen shown).
- Analysts:
  - Compare Nike to peers (e.g., Adidas, Puma, and other apparel retailers like Lululemon, American Eagle).
  - Build forecasts for revenue/EPS using data, industry signals, and seasonality.
  - Update **price targets** and **estimate trends** over time.

Key insight: estimates change
- The transcript notes that as time passes, analysts may **lower forecasts** for upcoming quarters (reflecting worsening conditions or new information).
---

concepts.title: 5) Seasonality: don’t compare the wrong quarters
concepts.body:
**Seasonality** means business performance changes predictably during the year.

Example logic from the video:
- End of year often has higher consumer spending (holidays).
- Early year can be weaker (returns, post-holiday slowdown).

Practical rule:
- Don’t only compare **quarter-to-quarter**.
- Often compare **year-over-year**: this quarter vs. the **same quarter last year**.

---

concepts.title: 6) Where to find earnings dates and reports (practical tools)
concepts.body:
The video gives several concrete ways to track earnings.

#### A) Earnings Whispers (weekly schedule)
- **Earnings Whispers** (Twitter / site) posts a weekly table of major companies reporting.
- Useful for seeing “what’s coming this week” (especially large-cap names).
- Reminder: the table may not include every company—only major ones.

#### B) Company website: Investor Relations (IR)
Every public company typically has an **Investor Relations (IR)** page where they must publish:
- Earnings date/time
- Press release / quarterly report
- Link to the earnings call webcast

How to find it quickly:
- Search: “[Company name] IR” or “[Ticker] IR”
- Example mentioned: “Wix IR” style search (works for most companies)

#### C) TradingView chart: the “E” icon
On **TradingView**, an **“E”** marker appears on the chart:
- Shows the **earnings date**
- Often shows **analyst expectations**
- Historical markers may show:
  - Green upward indicator = **beat**
  - Red downward indicator = **miss**
(Exact visuals depend on platform settings, but the concept is: the chart flags earnings events.)

#### D) Earnings timing: before open or after close
Earnings releases usually happen:
- **Before market open**, or
- **After market close**
Not during the middle of the trading day (generally).

---

concepts.title: 7) Earnings calls: the “numbers” + the “story”
concepts.body:
After the report is released, companies host an **earnings call** (investor call):
- Usually **30–60 minutes** after the press release
- Anyone can often join via the IR link (listen live)

Why calls matter:
- The “in-between” details (management tone, risks, demand trends, costs, inventory, FX impact) can be more important than the headline numbers.
- In this macro environment, investors are hunting for “threads” (signals) about inflation, demand, and future weakness/strength.

How professionals analyze calls:
- They read the **transcript** (written record).
- Platforms like Bloomberg can scan transcripts for keywords (example: count mentions of “inflation”).
- This helps detect what management is emphasizing or worried about.

---

concepts.title: 8) Volatility and the “binary event” nature of earnings
concepts.body:
The transcript calls earnings a **binary event**:
- It happens at a specific time/date.
- The stock can gap sharply up or down immediately after.

Why moves can be extreme:
- Even if revenue/EPS improve, the market may punish the stock if:
  - Expectations were higher
  - Guidance is lowered
  - One forward-looking sentence changes the outlook

Typical post-earnings drop sizes mentioned:
- **2%, 3%, 5%, 10%, even 20%** (or more)

#### Concrete example: Netflix
The video highlights Netflix to show that “beat” does not guarantee a rally.
- Netflix experienced very large drops after earnings:
  - One instance: about **-35%** after earnings (described in the transcript)
  - Another quarter: about **-21.79%**
- The chart indicator showed a “beat” (green), yet the stock still fell.
- Reason given: management said they **expect subscriber declines** (a forward-looking negative signal).
- Lesson: **Guidance and narrative can override the beat/miss label.**

---

concepts.title: 9) Can you profit from earnings season?
concepts.body:
The video’s message is cautious:
- Earnings can be exciting and tradable, but outcomes are hard to predict.
- Even services that try to estimate reactions (like Earnings Whispers) are still dealing with uncertainty.
- Because it’s a **high-volatility event**, it can be dangerous if you don’t manage risk.

Practical implication (from the logic presented):
- Treat earnings like a special risk event in your plan:
  - Either avoid holding through it,
  - Or size smaller / hedge,
  - Or accept that large gaps can happen regardless of “good results.”

---

takeaways.text: Track earnings dates for your holdings:
Check **Investor Relations (IR)** page
Use **TradingView “E”** markers
Review weekly calendars like **Earnings Whispers**
Know the core metrics the market grades:
**Revenue (top line)** vs estimates
**EPS (bottom line)** vs estimates
**Free cash flow**
**Guidance** (often the biggest driver)
Listen/read beyond the headline:
Read the press release and/or **earnings call transcript**
Watch for forward-looking statements about demand, FX (strong dollar), inflation, supply chain
Expect gaps:
Earnings is a **binary event**; stocks can move **2–20%+** quickly
Don’t assume “beat = up”:
Netflix example: beat indicators still preceded major drops due to negative outlook
Compare correctly:
Use **year-over-year** comparisons when seasonality matters, not only quarter-to-quarter

glossary.definition: The few-week period when many public companies report quarterly results.
A 3-month reporting period; companies report financial performance each quarter.
**Revenue / sales** (the first major line on the income statement).
**Net income / profit**, often discussed as **EPS**.
Profit allocated per share; a common profitability metric.
Cash generated after operating costs and capital expenditures; indicates financial flexibility.
Management’s forward-looking forecast (next quarter/year expectations).
Wall Street forecasts for revenue/EPS used as the benchmark for “beat/miss.”
The company’s official portal for earnings releases, calls, and investor materials.
Management discussion/Q&A after results; provides context and forward-looking commentary.
Written record of the earnings call.
An event with a discrete outcome at a specific time that can cause a sharp price jump up or down.

title: Earnings Season Rules: How to Read Reports, Where to Find Them, and Why Trading Them Is (Usually) a Gamble
learn_items: The 3 core numbers the market reacts to in every **earnings report**: **Revenue**, **EPS**, and **Guidance**
Where to reliably find official earnings information (and when it’s released)
Why trading right around earnings is effectively **high-risk speculation** due to algorithms, headlines, and sentiment parsing
Practical ways to track earnings dates and interpret post-earnings volatility on charts

concepts.title: 1) What “Earnings Season” Is (and why it matters)
concepts.body:
- **Earnings season** happens every quarter (every 3 months).
- Public companies are **required** to report results to investors—this is part of the “deal” of being publicly traded.
- The video highlights the scale using the **S&P 500**:
  - In the next **30 days**, about **403 out of 500** S&P 500 companies report.
  - That’s roughly **80%** of the index reporting in one month → a major driver of market-wide volatility.

**Why it matters:**
- This is one of the only recurring times you get a concentrated, structured “truth moment” about business performance across the market.
- Many stocks can move sharply in minutes because new information hits the market all at once.

---

concepts.title: 2) The 3 numbers the market cares about most
concepts.body:
Companies report many metrics, but the market (and trading algorithms) often react first to three headline items:

#### A) **Revenue (Sales)**
- **Revenue** = total sales / money brought in.
- Think: “How much did they sell?” “How many orders came in?”

#### B) **EPS (Earnings Per Share)**
- **EPS** = profit (or loss) divided by the number of shares.
- It standardizes profitability so investors can compare companies more easily.

> The transcript mentions EBITDA appears sometimes, but emphasizes that many traders focus heavily on **EPS**.

#### C) **Guidance (Forward Outlook / Forecast)**
- **Guidance** = management’s forecast for future revenue/profit (often next quarter).
- Markets are forward-looking: guidance can move a stock even more than the past quarter’s results.

---

concepts.title: 3) “Beat vs. Miss” and the idea of a “Triple Beat”
concepts.body:
Markets compare reported numbers to **expectations** (what “the market” predicted).

- **Beat** = reported number is better than expected  
- **Miss** = reported number is worse than expected  
- **In-line** = matches expectations

#### Example from the transcript (Monday.com / “MNDY” example)
- **Revenue:** reported ~**149.92** vs expected ~**141.2** → beat
- **EPS:** expected a **loss of -$0.37**, but reported **+$0.44** → beat
- **Guidance:** guided **154–156** vs expected **150** → beat

This is called a **Triple Beat**:
1) Beat on revenue  
2) Beat on EPS  
3) Beat (or raised) guidance  

**Strategy concept mentioned:**
- Some strategies buy only companies that deliver a **triple beat** (revenue + EPS + guidance all better than expected).

---

concepts.title: 4) Where to find earnings reports (official sources)
concepts.body:
Every US-listed company has an **Investor Relations (IR)** website.

How to find it:
- Search: **“CompanyName IR”** (example: “Apple IR”)
- Or: “CompanyName investor relations”

What you can find there:
- **Press release** with headline results
- **Financial statements**
- Segment breakdowns (e.g., Apple revenue by category)
- The earnings call details (webcast link, transcript later)

---

concepts.title: 5) When earnings are released (timing)
concepts.body:
Earnings are typically released either:

#### A) **Before market open**
- Often around **6:00–8:00 AM US time**
- The speaker converts this to roughly **1:00–4:00 PM Israel time** (adding ~7 hours)

#### B) **After market close**
- Could be **5–30 minutes** after the close (varies by company)

**Important mechanic:**
- Once released, the company posts it immediately on its IR site—and the market reacts immediately.

---

concepts.title: 6) Why earnings trading is “100% a gamble” (in the speaker’s view)
concepts.body:
The speaker’s core rule:
- Trading earnings reactions is **not** 99% gambling—it's **100% gambling**, because outcomes are not reliably predictable even if you know the numbers.

Reasons:
- Behind the headline numbers are many sub-metrics (margins, segments, costs, regional performance).
- The **press release language** and CEO/CFO commentary can change sentiment.
- The market reaction depends on:
  - What was already priced in
  - Guidance tone
  - Hidden weak spots (or strengths)
  - Broader macro conditions (rates, recession fears, banking stress)

#### Algorithms dominate short-term reaction
- The transcript claims roughly **70% of trading** is driven by computers/algorithms.
- Algorithms:
  - Parse numbers instantly (revenue/EPS/guidance vs expectations)
  - Parse language sentiment (“sounds good/bad”)
  - React to analyst upgrades/downgrades after the call

**Practical implication:**
- Even “good” results can lead to a drop if expectations were higher, guidance disappoints, or wording signals caution.

---

concepts.title: 7) The earnings call: the second volatility event
concepts.body:
Typically **30–60 minutes** after the report:
- Management holds an **earnings call** (webcast/Zoom/YouTube/etc.).
- It’s mostly a management presentation + analyst Q&A (varies by company).

Why it matters:
- New information appears: competition, demand weakness/strength, layoffs, pricing pressure, cloud spending, etc.
- This can cause a *second wave* of price movement after the initial headline reaction.

---

concepts.title: 8) Analysts and price targets: another catalyst
concepts.body:
After earnings (sometimes within hours):
- Analysts update **price targets** (e.g., raising Apple from 210 to 240—example given).
- They may also issue “sell/avoid” notes if guidance is weak.

Why you should care:
- Some traders follow analysts with strong historical accuracy (the transcript mentions the idea of an analyst with ~**80%** success rate).
- Algorithms can also trade off these analyst updates.

---

concepts.title: 9) How to track earnings dates and upcoming reporters
concepts.body:
Tools mentioned:

#### A) Earnings calendars (example site mentioned: “Koyfin”)
- Used to see how many S&P 500 companies report in the next 30 days.

#### B) **TradingView** events on charts
- TradingView can mark earnings dates and show whether EPS beat/missed.
- Useful for studying how a stock historically reacts around earnings.

#### C) Investor Relations announcements
- The company IR site posts the earnings date once scheduled.

#### D) Social/feeds for fast headlines
- “Earnings Whispers” (Twitter) posts weekly schedules.
- Twitter accounts that auto-post Bloomberg/terminal headlines can deliver results immediately (the key idea: speed of information).

---

concepts.title: 10) Reading earnings through charts: gaps and post-earnings moves
concepts.body:
Earnings often cause **gaps** and sharp moves.

#### Key term: **Gap Up / Gap Down**
- **Gap-up**: stock opens significantly higher than the prior close after news.
- **Gap-down**: opens significantly lower.

Examples from the transcript:
- **Nvidia (NVDA)** had an earnings event where it **gapped up ~+14%**.
- **Meta (Facebook)** had a dramatic earnings drop of about **-26%** immediately after earnings (a “gap” that can’t be “fixed” like a normal dip—his metaphor: not something a dentist can repair).

**Lesson:**
- Earnings can create instant moves of **10%–30%** in minutes.
- Stocks can whipsaw: down 10% then up 20%, or up 10% then down 20%.

---

concepts.title: 11) Options market: “implied move” (conceptual mention)
concepts.body:
The speaker notes:
- The **options market** “prices” expected volatility around earnings.
- Practically, traders look at options to estimate the market’s expected move (often called the **implied move**).

**Use for investors:**
- If options imply a very large move, it’s a warning that earnings is a high-volatility event.

---

concepts.title: 12) Using transcripts to detect themes (AI, layoffs, recession)
concepts.body:
A more advanced insight:
- Computers scan earnings call transcripts for specific keywords and themes.

Example given:
- Counting mentions of **“AI”**:
  - Apple mentioned “AI” very little in a prior call (the speaker implies ~0–1 meaningful mentions).
  - Nvidia’s transcript contained many “AI” mentions.

Why it matters:
- Keyword frequency can signal what management is emphasizing (growth drivers, risks, cost cuts).
- Across earnings season, you can track macro themes:
  - **Layoffs**
  - **Recession / slowdown**
  - **Banking stress**
  - **Interest rates**
  - **AI investment**

---

takeaways.text: Track every holding’s earnings date:
Check the company’s **Investor Relations (IR)** page
Confirm on **TradingView** (or an earnings calendar)
For every earnings report, focus first on:
Treat trading around earnings as high-risk:
Expect sudden **gaps** and **10%–30%** moves in extreme cases
Remember algorithms react to both numbers *and* wording/sentiment
Study historical earnings reactions on the chart:
Look for repeated patterns: gap-ups, gap-downs, reversals
Consider a “quality filter” approach instead of gambling:
Example filter mentioned: buy only **triple beat** companies (revenue + EPS + guidance all beat)

glossary.definition: The quarterly period when most public companies report results.
Total sales/income from operations.
Profit divided by shares outstanding.
Management’s forecast for future performance (often next quarter).
Results better than / worse than / equal to expectations.
Revenue beat + EPS beat + guidance beat/raised.
Company website section with official financial reports and filings.
Management discussion/Q&A after results; can move the stock.
Price jump between prior close and next open due to news.
The market’s expected price move around earnings derived from options pricing.

title: Earnings Season (Quarterly Reports): How It Works, What to Watch, and How to Prepare
learn_items: How **earnings season** works and why it can move stocks sharply (often outside regular trading hours).
The 3 core metrics markets judge: **EPS**, **revenue**, and **guidance**—and what a “triple beat” means.
How to find your company’s earnings date using **Investor Relations (IR)** pages and chart symbols.
Why trading *into* earnings is risky (gaps, thin after-hours liquidity, stops not behaving as expected).

concepts.title: 1) What “Earnings Season” Is (and why it matters)
concepts.body:
- **Earnings season** is the period each quarter when public companies release their financial results.
- It’s one of the most market-moving times because companies don’t just publish numbers—they also explain the *story behind the numbers*:
  - What drove growth or weakness
  - What trends management sees ahead
  - New themes the market cares about (examples mentioned):
    - Retail companies discussing **shrinkage** (inventory loss/theft)  
          - Tech companies emphasizing **AI** (and investors even track how often “AI” is mentioned)

concepts.title: 2) Quarterly structure: Q1–Q4 (and why the months matter)
concepts.body:
Most companies report on a standard calendar:
- **Q1:** Jan–Mar  
- **Q2:** Apr–Jun  
- **Q3:** Jul–Sep  
- **Q4:** Oct–Dec  

Why this matters:
- A Q3 report is *not* year-end, but companies often start shaping expectations for the next year.
- Knowing which months are included helps you interpret seasonality (e.g., summer vs. holiday quarter).

concepts.title: 3) The “Big 3” metrics the market focuses on
concepts.body:
Even though reports include many line items, the video emphasizes three headline drivers:

1) **Revenue (Sales) / “Top line”**
- The total money coming in.
- Often shown as “revenue” or “sales.”

2) **EPS (Earnings Per Share)**
- Profitability per share.
- Commonly compared to expectations.

3) **Guidance (Forward outlook / forecasts)**
- Management’s expectations for upcoming quarters (and sometimes the year).
- Guidance can move the stock even more than the past quarter’s results.

#### Expectations are everything
- Results are judged versus **consensus estimates** (aggregated analyst forecasts).
- If there are few/no analysts, markets may rely more on the company’s own prior guidance.

concepts.title: 4) “Beat / Miss” and the idea of a “Triple Beat”
concepts.body:
- A company can:
  - **Beat** expectations (do better than expected)
  - **Miss** expectations (do worse than expected)

The video highlights a common earnings-season shorthand:
- **“Triple beat”** = beats on all three:
  - **EPS beat**
  - **Revenue beat**
  - **Guidance raised / better-than-expected outlook**
  - The opposite can happen too:
  - Missing on EPS + revenue + guidance is typically punished heavily.

Important nuance from the instructor:
- A “good report” is not what the CEO says—it’s what the **market reaction** says.  
    - Practically: for a long investor, a “good earnings report” is one where the **stock rises** after the release.

concepts.title: 5) How to find when a company reports earnings (practical methods)
concepts.body:
#### Method A: Use the company’s Investor Relations (IR) page
- Search: **“[Company] IR”** or **“[Company] investor relations”**
- Example from the transcript:
  - Search **“AMD IR”** → go to AMD’s Investor Relations site → look for the earnings release date once announced.  
      - Search **“Netflix IR”** → Netflix shows the earnings date (example given: **October 18**) and often provides:
        - Add-to-calendar links
    - Webcast / call details

Why IR matters:
- It’s the company’s official source for:
  - Earnings release timing
  - Press release documents
  - Webcast links and transcripts

#### Method B: Use chart/market platforms that mark earnings with an “E”
- Many charting tools show an **“E” icon** on the chart at the earnings date.
- The instructor demonstrates this on a chart for Bank of America:
  - The “E” marker indicates the upcoming earnings date
  - Platforms often show expected **EPS** and expected **revenue** near that date

concepts.title: 6) The earnings release timeline (“the chain”)
concepts.body:
A typical earnings event has a predictable sequence:

1) **Earnings release time**
- Usually either:
  - **Before market open** (pre-market)
  - **After market close** (after-hours)
- It’s scheduled in advance (not random).

2) **Official results document**
- Companies publish a formal release with financial statements/metrics.
- The instructor stresses reporting is regulated:
  - False reporting can lead to severe legal consequences.

3) **Earnings call (investor call)**
- Happens typically **30–90 minutes after** the release.
- Management speaks:
  - CEO narrative (“what happened”)
  - CFO financial detail (“how it happened”)
- Analysts may ask questions.
- This call can *change* the stock’s direction even if the initial reaction was strong.

concepts.title: 7) After-hours / pre-market trading: why it’s dangerous
concepts.body:
The video highlights **after-hours trading** (and pre-market) as a key risk zone:

- **After-hours liquidity is thinner**
    - Fewer shares available
  - Wider spreads
  - Faster, more violent moves

- **Price can gap instantly**
    - Example scenario: stock misses → drops **10% in seconds**
  - You may not be able to react in time.

#### Stop-loss problem around earnings
- The instructor warns that in fast after-hours moves:
  - A regular **stop-loss** may not protect you the way you expect.
  - Gaps can “skip over” your stop level.
- He mentions **stop-limit** as a different order type (with different behavior), implying you must understand order mechanics before relying on stops during earnings.

concepts.title: 8) Why the stock can reverse after the initial move
concepts.body:
A major lesson: the first move is not always the final move.

Common patterns described:

#### Pattern A: Bad initial reaction → recovery during/after the call
- Stock drops hard on headline miss (e.g., -10%).
- Investors read deeper, then listen to the call.
- Management explains:
  - The weak quarter was temporary
  - Next quarter outlook is strong
  - Provides evidence (pipeline, demand, resolved issues, etc.)
- Stock may rebound significantly.

#### Pattern B: Good initial reaction → collapse after management speaks
- Stock rises on headline beat (e.g., +5%).
- Then CEO/CFO commentary disappoints (weak guidance, worrying trend, margin pressure).
- Stock can reverse sharply downward.

concepts.title: 9) Trading rule from the instructor: don’t “gamble” earnings
concepts.body:
Core stance stated clearly:
- He personally **does not trade the earnings event itself** (“I don’t trade earnings reports”).
- Reason: outcomes depend on market expectations and reaction, not just “good numbers.”

Key logic:
- A company can report “good” results but still fall if:
  - Growth is slower than peers
  - Guidance is reduced
  - The market expected even more

concepts.title: 10) Position management: trading vs. long-term holding into earnings
concepts.body:
He distinguishes between:
- **Short-term trades**: may reduce risk or exit before earnings to avoid event volatility.
- **Large, long-term holdings** (examples: big tech like Google/Tesla mentioned):
  - He is less likely to sell just because earnings are coming
  - Because the position is part of a longer-term plan (implied: volatility is tolerated)

takeaways.text: Know the calendar:
Identify whether your stock reports **pre-market** or **after-hours**.
Track the “Big 3”:
**Revenue**, **EPS**, **guidance** vs **expectations**.
Look for “triple beat” / “triple miss” dynamics:
EPS + revenue + guidance together often drive the biggest moves.
Use official sources:
Check the company’s **Investor Relations (IR)** page for the confirmed earnings date and webcast.
Respect after-hours mechanics:
Liquidity is thinner; moves can be instant; spreads wider.
Don’t assume a **stop-loss** will save you during an earnings gap.
Don’t confuse “good company” with “good earnings reaction”:
The market decides what’s “good” based on expectations and forward outlook.
If you feel emotionally off or stressed:
Consider not trading—earnings volatility punishes poor decision-making.

glossary.definition: The recurring period when many companies report quarterly results.
Three-month reporting periods used for financial statements.
Total income from selling products/services.
Profit divided by shares outstanding; a key profitability metric.
Management’s forecast for future revenue/EPS or business conditions.
The average/combined expectations of analysts covering the stock.
Reporting above/below expected EPS/revenue/guidance.
Beating expectations on **EPS + revenue + guidance**.
Trading outside regular market hours; typically lower liquidity and higher volatility.
The company’s official portal for earnings releases, filings, and investor communications.
A scheduled conference call where management discusses results and answers analyst questions.
Order types intended to manage downside risk; can behave unpredictably during gaps and thin liquidity.

title: Market “Slogans” That Actually Teach You How to Trade and Invest (Trend, Falling Knife, Risk, and P/E)
learn_items: How to use **trend-following** correctly by matching the trend to your **timeframe**
Why “**don’t catch a falling knife**” is mostly about waiting for confirmation and managing risk (or using a stop)
When “**never invest money you can’t afford to lose**” is good advice—and when it’s misleading (index investing vs. speculation)
How to think about **P/E (Price-to-Earnings)** properly (growth vs. “cheap” value traps), with real company examples

concepts.title: 1) “The trend is your friend (till the end)” — but *which* trend?
concepts.body:
**Core idea:** Price trends exist, but they depend on the **timeframe** you’re looking at.

- A stock can be in a:
  - **Long-term downtrend** (monthly/weekly chart)
  - While also having **short-term uptrends** (daily/hourly chart)
- The speaker shows this using **BABA (Alibaba)**:
    - On higher timeframes (daily/weekly/monthly), the trend looks clearly **down**
  - But zooming in (even to hourly), there are periods where the trend is **up for several days**
- Practical meaning:
  - If you’re a **long-term investor**, you should care more about the **weekly/monthly** trend and business fundamentals.
  - If you’re a **swing trader** (1–3 days), you may trade short up-moves even inside a bigger downtrend.

**Actionable rule:**  
Before entering any trade/investment, define your **holding period** first. Then analyze trend on the matching chart:
- Investor (months/years): weekly/monthly trend
- Swing trader (days/weeks): daily trend
- Day trader (intraday): hourly/minute trend

---

concepts.title: 2) “Don’t catch a falling knife” — avoid buying just because it dropped
concepts.body:
**Core idea:** A stock that keeps falling can keep falling. Buying “because it’s cheaper” is not a strategy.

- The speaker describes the common trap:
    - Price drops → looks like a bargain → you buy → it drops again → repeat
- Examples mentioned as “hope traps”:
    - **BABA**, **NIO**, **Lucid** (as illustrations of repeated declines that tempt buyers)

#### What to do instead: wait for evidence of a reversal
He suggests waiting until the stock starts to:
- **Change direction**
- Create **new highs** (a sign momentum may be shifting)
- Form a **bottom** and then move upward (confirmation)

He uses **ITB** (a housing-related ETF) as an example:
- Trend is down, but some investors may start building a position when they see signs it’s turning.
- Important warning: even if it starts rising, it can still “fail” and drop again.

#### When it *can* be okay to buy a falling stock
It depends on:
- Who you are (trader vs. investor)
- Your timeframe
- Whether you use a **stop-loss**
- Whether you’ve done real **valuation** and understand the business

**Stop-loss example from the video:**
- ITB around **58.8**
- Set a stop around **57**
- If it breaks down again, you exit—because the “trend friend” relationship ended.

**Key lesson:**  
“Don’t catch a falling knife” is really about **risk management + confirmation**, not about never buying dips.

---

concepts.title: 3) “Never invest money you can’t afford to lose” — true for speculation, less true for diversified investing
concepts.body:
**Core idea:** This slogan assumes the stock market is a casino. That’s sometimes accurate—but not always.

#### When the slogan is good advice (casino-like behavior)
It’s highly relevant when you invest in:
- Companies with **no real business yet**
- “Future plans” and **promises** instead of results
- Firms with ongoing losses and unclear path to profitability
- Tiny stocks where you’re betting on a single event (e.g., “drug approval rumor”)
- Anything you don’t understand (outsourcing your judgment to hype)

In those cases, yes: you could lose most or all of your money.

#### When the slogan is misleading (long-term diversified investing)
He contrasts speculation with investing in the **S&P 500** (500 large US companies).

- If you invest broadly via an **index fund/ETF**, you’re not betting on one company.
